title: YAY v YAZ
counsel: Renee Oei (Anthony Law Corporation) for the plaintiff
Tan Wee Tim Cheryl and Yeo Wei Ern (Kalco Law LLC) for the defendant.
html:
YAY v YAZ

YAY v YAZ
[2026] SGFC 43

Case Number:FC/D 4925/2023
Decision Date:26 March 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Chua Wei Yuan
Counsel Name(s): Renee Oei (Anthony Law Corporation) for the plaintiff; Tan Wee Tim Cheryl and Yeo Wei Ern (Kalco Law LLC) for the defendant.
Parties: YAY — YAZ

Family – Care and Control

Family – Maintenance – Children

Family – Division of Matrimonial Assets

26 March 2026

Judgment reserved.

District Judge Chua Wei Yuan:

1       These are the reasons for my decision on the ancillary matters in FC/D 4925/2023.

2       This matter involves a plaintiff husband (“H”) and defendant wife (“W”), who have a daughter (“D”) who has turned 6 this year.

3       This claim was filed in October 2023. Interim judgment (“IJ”) was granted on an uncontested basis in March 2024, and the ancillary matters hearing (“AM”) was fixed for hearing in August 2025.

Children’s Issues

Custody

4       H sought joint custody of D, whereas W submitted that she should receive sole custody of D or, in the alternative, a veto power on custody issues in the event parties cannot agree.

5       I ordered that parties have joint custody of D.

6       W’s argument for sole custody is based on CX v CY [2005] 3 SLR(R) 690 at [38], where Lai Siu Chiu J (delivering the judgment of the Court of Appeal) had explained:

38    We would emphasise that recent decisions have been inclined towards making joint or no custody orders due to the need to ensure that the child becomes attached to both parents. The idea behind joint or no custody orders is to ensure that neither parent has a better right over the child and that both have a responsibility to bring the child up in the best way possible. Similarly, the child has a right to the guidance of both his parents. Parenthood is a lifelong responsibility and does not end at a particular age of the child, but continues until the child reaches adulthood. The question we have to answer will always be what is best for the child in the future. We agree with Assoc Prof Debbie Ong that the exceptional circumstances where sole custody orders are made may be where one parent physically, sexually, or emotionally abuses the child (see Debbie Ong, “Making No Custody Order” ([19] supra) at p 586), or where the relationship of the parties is such that co-operation is impossible even after the avenues of mediation and counselling have been explored, and the lack of co-operation is harmful to the child (see Debbie Ong, “Parents and Custody Orders” ([27] supra) at p 222–223).

7       W submits that H is facing ongoing criminal proceedings, where he has been charged with a total of 10 counts of voyeurism and outrage of modesty. While D was not a victim in the alleged offences, W submitted that the nature of the offences was concerning in the light of the fact that D is a young girl, and that H would if he is incarcerated be uncontactable and unable to provide his input as a joint custodial parent. W also highlighted that, if H is incarcerated, there is no guarantee that he, as a Malaysian national, will be allowed to remain in Singapore; if H has to leave Singapore, joint custody will be even more difficult in practice.

8       I am unable to agree with these submissions.

9       First, the general principle is that parents should have joint custody of the child. The threshold to make a sole custody order is an exceptional one. The exception to a joint custody order recognised in CX v CY is where the parent physically, sexually or emotionally abuses the child. That is not the allegation in this case. In fact, H has not been convicted of the offences with which he has been charged. It appears that H is in fact contesting the charges. It is therefore unsafe to proceed as if H is guilty of those offences.

10     W had alleged that H had secretly filmed D while she was naked in the toilet and continue doing so after D told him not to, and that H had done something about D’s private part. I do not make much of these allegations. The allegation that H had done something about D’s private part was promptly denied and, more importantly, had no particulars as to what was done or when it was done. As for H filming D, H said that this was a distortion of the facts. H claimed that he took a video of D on the potty because H found the sounds that D was making to be funny and amusing. Although he continued despite D’s protests, H says that D’s private parts were not captured in the video, and that H had promptly sent the video to W. I agree with H’s submissions that if the video were as compromising as W claims, not only would it have been strange for H to send it to W, but W should also have demonstrated that the video was not innocent.

11     As for a veto power, it was clarified in WZF v WZG [2025] SGHCF 1 that a “veto power” should not be generally granted because it serves to significantly alter the dynamics of the relationship towards a hierarchy where one parent is subordinate rather than an equal contributor. The exception, as recognised in TEN v TEO, is where parties were so hostile towards each other that they had to resort to multiple applications to the court over several years, such that the High Court affirmed the Family Court’s decision to allow the parent given care and control to make major decisions if parties were unable to agree. Even so, the order would require consultation and keeping the other parent abreast of developments.

12     In this case, the concern is not so much that parties are hostile to the extent that cooperation is impossible, but that H may become an absent parent if he is incarcerated and/or disallowed from remaining in Singapore. I do not think this presents a serious concern. As explained by Woo Bih Li J in YG v YH [2008] SGHC 166 at [9], cited in WZF v WZG at [20]:

… although parties who have been awarded joint custody of their children in divorce proceedings do have certain rights as part of that joint custody, the parties should take a sensible approach towards the exercise of those rights. It is not in the interest of the children for the parents to be overly insistent on or calculative in respect of their rights. … The Wife does not have to consult the Husband on every minor health issue of the children, eg, cold, cough and fever, but is to consult the Husband on every intended hospital admission or hospital procedure unless it is not possible to do so, as in the case of an emergency. If any of the children is hospitalised on an emergency basis, the party having physical care of the child at the time of hospitalisation is to inform the other party as soon as is practicable, for example, within two hours of the start of the emergency.

[emphasis added by Court in WZF v WZG]

13     In other words, as the Court explained in WZF v WZG, the parent with care and control is perfectly entitled to make decisions in emergencies or where time is of the essence where the other parent is missing for some unknown reason. In the context of this case, it would mean that in the event that there are time-sensitive decisions to be made, then W would, without specific provision for a veto power in the court order, be entitled to make those decisions if H is incarcerated and unable to communicate with W in a timely manner.

14     For these reasons, I order that custody be joint.

Care and control

15     Both parties agreed that W would receive sole care and control.

16     In the event, I ordered that W have sole care and control of D.

Access

17     To give some context, H had not seen D for about 2 years since he moved out of the matrimonial home. As W had concerns about D interacting with H, arising from the long period of D and H’s absence from each other’s lives and from the charges which H was facing, parties had agreed that, without prejudice to the ancillary matters hearing, H would have supervised access through the DSSA. Presently, H has completed 4 of 8 access sessions scheduled.

18     W submitted that H should have continue to have supervised access, and that this should not progress to unsupervised access without clearance from a counsellor or a psychologist.

19     H, on the other hand, sought unsupervised access. H proposed the following scheme to govern access:

(a)    The Father to have access every Sunday from 10 am to 5 pm. The Father to give advance notice of at least forty-eight (48) hours if he is not exercising access.

(b)    In addition, the Father is to have at least one (1) weekday dinner with the Child from 6 pm to 8.30 pm. The Father will inform the Mother at least one week in advance which day he will exercise his access for weekday dinners. If Parties are not able to agree, the Father will have Friday dinners from 6 pm to 8.30 pm.

(c)    The Father is at liberty to request for more access subject to the Child’s schedule. The Mother will not withhold consent unreasonably.

(d)    For Chinese New Year (“CNY”), the Father is to have access as follows:

(i)    Commencing in 2026 and on even years thereafter, the Father to have CNY eve from after school or 2 pm (whichever is earlier) to 10 pm on the first day of CNY.

(ii)   Commencing in 2027 and on odd years thereafter, the Father to have the first day of CNY from 8.30 pm to 9 pm on the second day of CNY.

(e)    Save for CNY access, the Father to have alternate public holidays from 10 am to 8.30 pm. The Father’s public holiday access will start from Deepavali in 2025.

(f)    The Father to have half of the Child’s school holidays in March, June, September and November / December (“School Holidays”). Parties are to agree on which half. If parties are unable to agree, the Father will have the first half of School Holidays on odd years and the second half of the School Holidays on even years. Parties will rely on the MOE Calendar to determine the duration (and consequently, respective halves of the school holidays). The Mother is to hand over the Child’s passport to the Father on the day of the handover for School Holidays. The Father is to return the passport to the Mother on the day of handover after his School Holidays access.

(g)    Parties are at liberty to bring the Child overseas during their respective care and during the Father’s School Holiday access. The travelling parent will provide the following notices from the date of the intended travel to the other parent:

(i)    One week in advance when travelling to Johor Bahru, Malaysia; and

(ii)   One month in advance for any other countries or parts of Malaysia.

(h)    The travelling parent is to provide the other parent the flight details, including flight itinerary, accommodation, and contact information at least one (1) week in advance to the date of the intended travel.

(i)    For all access arrangements, Parties will conduct the handover of the Child at a public place, either at the void deck of the Mother’s residence or at a nearby mall.

(j)    Parties at liberty to mutually discuss and agree to any changes in the above access arrangements.

…

20     W submitted that it would be a big jump from supervised access at DSSA to unsupervised overnight and overseas access. Considering that H’s future was uncertain, it would be better for access to be monitored than granted wholesale, and increased incrementally (eg, progressing from supervised access at DSSA to supervised access in a less strict/regimented situation, and eventually to overseas/overnight access when D is older).

Whether access should be supervised

21     Supervised access ought to be the exception rather than the norm. In APE v APF [2015] SGHC 17 at [32] the Court stated that supervised access should be ordered only if the following exceptional circumstances are present:

(a)     there are serious welfare concerns regarding the non-custodial parent such as violence or inappropriate parenting if the child were left unattended with the non-custodial parent;

(b)     considering the state of the relationship between the non-custodial parent and the child before and after the commencement of the divorce proceedings, the child has been estranged from the non-custodial parent such that the parental-child relationship is in need of serious repair (eg, if the child is fearful in meeting the non-custodial parent); or

(c)     factors exist such that it is difficult for unsupervised access to be effectively implemented (eg, where the relationship between both parents is so acrimonious that the custodial parent frustrates the effectiveness of unsupervised access orders and unsupervised access is not possible without detriment to the child).

22     It is not alleged that the third circumstance is present here. As for the first (ie, inappropriate parenting), for the same reasons given for awarding joint custody—essentially that the criminal allegations against H are unproven and in any event do not pertain to D—I think they are not a sufficient ground to order supervised access. However, the state of the relationship is, in my view, a relevant factor. H argues that he has not had contact with D because W has alienated them since 2023. The argument, it seems, is that W would be in a sense profiting from her own wrong if she were to seek supervised access on the basis of the rift in the relationship between H and D.

23     In my view, the ultimate concern still has to be the best interests of D (and not the best interests of the parents). Even if W had contributed to the alienation between D and H, the point is that D presently requires some time to warm up to H. For this reason, I consider it most appropriate for the current tranche of supervised access at DSSA to continue. After that, there is to be a review of access arrangements and, unless there is any adverse report, H ought to be able to enjoy unsupervised access.

Terms of access

24     W did not make detailed submissions on the terms of unsupervised access. In oral submissions, W did not take issue with the terms of access sought by H.

25     However, I do not consider it appropriate for the Court to make an order that H be at liberty to request for more access subject to D’s schedule, and that W shall not withhold consent unreasonably. This, in my judgment, would put the order at risk of unnecessary challenges in future, which risk is substantial considering W’s position that access ought to be supervised in the first place.

Orders on access

26     In summary, my orders for access are as follows:

(a)     H is to continue having supervised access to D, until successful completion of the existing tranche of supervised access at DSSA.

(b)     Upon completion of the existing tranche of supervised access at DSSA, H is to have unsupervised access to D on the following terms, unless parties otherwise agree:

(i)       Subject to the exceptions below, H shall have access to D:

(A)       for dinner on Fridays from 6.00pm to 8.30pm; and

(B)       on Sundays from 10.00am to 5.00pm

(ii)       For the period from the eve to the 2nd day of Chinese New Year, H shall have access to D only:

(A)       in years ending with an even number, from 2.00pm or the end of D’s school day (if it is a school day) on the eve of Chinese New Year, to 10.00pm on the 1st day of Chinese New Year; and

(B)       in years ending with an odd number, from 8.30pm on the 1st day of Chinese New Year to 9.00pm on the 2nd day of Chinese New Year.

(iii)       H shall have access to D from 10.00am to 8.30pm on alternate public holidays.

(iv)       H shall have access to D, during D’s March, June, September and November/December school holidays (determined in accordance with the Ministry of Education’s calendar) (“holiday access”), for:

(A)       in years ending with an even number, the first half; and

(B)       in years ending with an odd number, the second half.

(v)       W shall hand H D’s passport for the duration of each holiday access.

(vi)       H may bring D overseas during holiday access, subject to notice to W:

(A)       of the intended location:

(I)       1 week in advance of the date of travel, when travelling to Johor Bahru, Malaysia; and

(II)     1 month in advance of the date of travel in all other cases; and

(B)       of the flight itinerary (or equivalent), accommodation details, and contact information, 1 week in advance of the date of travel.

(vii)       Parties shall hand D over at the void deck of W’s residence.

(c)     Liberty to apply.

Further orders

27     H sought these further orders as part of the child’s arrangements:

(k)    Parties shall refrain from making disparaging and demeaning remarks about each other in the presence of the Child.

(l)    Parties shall not disclose court papers from any court proceedings involving Parties to the Child.

(m)    Parties shall not introduce romantic third parties or male strangers (who are not family members) to the Child without the other party’s written consent or until the Child turns 12 years old.

28     W was prepared to consent to the proposed further orders (k) and (l). However, W submitted that (m) was inappropriate and ought not be part of the order.

29     I record orders (k) and (l), but only on the basis these are consented to by parties.

30     However, I do not make any order on proposed order (m). H’s concern was that W had brought D to social gatherings with men whom she described on social media as “male mistresses”, and had drank with them. H says that, essentially, neither party ought to keep introducing romantic partners to D because it would be destabilising for the child, unless they are “stable” romantic partners. However, I am unable to agree with this. The proposed order makes no mention of the word “stable”, but even if it had, it would beg the question of what stable means. More fundamentally, I am inclined to agree with W that H may be over-interpreting the social media posts, and that In any event, the introduction of new romantic partners is a matter properly for parties to decide on and not for the court to interfere. Having said that, I wish to impress upon both parents the importance of their role in educating D on what healthy relationships and appropriate boundaries entail. While I have not made any order in this regard, it behoves parties to exercise great care and common sense in deciding what to expose D to.

Maintenance

Spousal maintenance

31     Both parties agree that W shall receive no spousal maintenance.

Child maintenance

32     Broadly, W seeks an order that H pay for ¼ of D’s monthly expenses, which she says totals $3,267.32 (ie, about $817 per month). W also seeks an order that the first 5 years of maintenance be paid as a lump sum, to be deducted from H’s share of matrimonial assets upon division. This, W says, is to address the contingency that H may be imprisoned, and the attendant difficulty of getting maintenance from him.

33     On the other hand, H says that he ought to pay only $200.00 per month as child maintenance.

Child’s reasonable expenses

34     Most of D’s expenses were agreed upon. I will address the disputed ones.

35     At the hearing, W was prepared to proceed on the basis that disputed items for the share of household expenses were resolved in H’s favour. In other words, the total household expenses excluding mortgage repayments would be $1,643.00. H did not dispute the proposition that household expenses should be attributed to W and D in equal shares. For this reason, I consider that D’s share of household expenses would be quantified at $821.50 (subject to my next point).

36     H disputes the $150.00 which W budgeted for other miscellaneous expenses. W explained that this sum was to be used for electronic devices and features such as Google Home. H, on the other hand, says that these are not reasonable financial needs. I can accept in principle that a small sum should be budgeted every month for miscellaneous expenses. However, the budget of $150.00 per month would mean that W would spend $1,800.00 a year on such expenses. This, in my view, is excessive. These are bordering on luxuries or at least discretionary spending, and I consider that a reasonable ceiling for such miscellaneous expenses should be $25.00 per month (or $300.00 per year)—even if this does not get W very far, it is for W to supplement this budget out of her own pocket if she wishes to enjoy these kinds of features at home. Further, I consider that expenses like these are incurred for the benefit of the entire household and not merely for D. In the event, this expense is reclassified as a household expense for which only a 50% share is to be attributed to D. This would bring D’s share of household expenses to $834.00 per month.

37     Next, H disputes D’s medical expenses. W had quantified these expenses at $80 per month. This would mean that W spends $960 a year on D’s medical expenses. I agree with H that this is excessive in the absence of documentary support, and that a reasonable expense would be $20.00 per month.

38     Next, H disputes D’s kindergarten school fees, which W quantified at $730.00 per month. H takes issue with the fact that there are no receipts for this expense, and that this sum is inflated because D would have enjoyed government subsidies in respect of this sum. In my view, even in the absence of supporting documentation, $730 is a fair monthly expense for kindergarten. Although H has claimed that W would have enjoyed government subsidies, H has not particularised what these government subsidies ought to be, or how much they ought to be worth, even though it would be entirely open to H to take at least a prima facie view on them. In the event, I allow this entire expense as being reasonable.

39     H originally took issue with the school expenses of $80.00 per month. Considering the time at which I heard parties on the ancillary matters (ie, D would almost have started primary school at the time this hearing concluded), I directed parties to address me, by way of a supplementary affidavit, on what the school expenses are expected to be. In the parties’ supplementary affidavit, W quantified school expenses at about $485 per month, while H quantified school expenses at about $109 per month. In the event, H really has no issue to take with W’s initial quantification of $80 per month in her fact and position sheet. I will proceed on the basis that school expenses are $80 per month.

40     H takes issue with D’s cost of transportation, which W quantified at $300.00 per month without supporting documentation. In my view, this sum appears somewhat excessive, especially given that D would be entitled to concessionary travel. If D has been taking, for example, private hire vehicles or taxis, the frequency and necessity of this has not been made clear. In the event, I allow transport expenses at $50.00 per month.

41     Finally, H takes issue with entertainment, hobbies & sports, and tours/family outings, which were quantified at $250.00, $50.00 and $300.00 per month, respectively. H’s point is that these are luxuries and therefore ought not to be allowed. In my view, some amount of entertainment and outings are normal and reasonable and to be expected as part of D’s development. Accordingly, I do not think that it is fair to budget nothing at all for these expenses and to regard them wholly as luxuries. In my view, it would be fair to budget $50.00 per month on these in total.

42     In summary, I consider that D’s total monthly expenses are approximately $2,257, and that her total expenses including her share of household expenses are approximately $3,091.

H’s share of payment

43     The parties’ respective incomes were not seriously disputed at the hearing. Based on the parties’ latest notice of assessment, H earned a take-home income of $21,700 per year, while W earned almost $98,000 that same year. In other words, H has earned about 18% of the W and H’s aggregate earnings.

44     H sought to argue that W was in fact earning more than she had declared in her notice of assessment, because she had not only quantified her expenses at some $13,000 per month (ie, more than her take-home income of about $8,100 per month), but she had spent some $10,000 on one of her credit cards in a single month. However, it appears to me that W has a sizeable amount of savings, and the fact that she has spent more than her take-home income is not conclusive of the proposition that she is earning more than she is reporting. I am not persuaded that there is a sustained and significant over-spending (relative to reported income) of a nature as to invite the inference that income was under-reported. I am not entirely inclined to infer from her list of expenses that she was under-reporting income (in my view, it is more likely that the expenses were over-estimated). Nonetheless, I can conclude more generally that W is able to live comfortably with her income and savings combined.

45     H also sought to persuade me that $200.00 was all he could afford in maintenance, after deducting all his other expenses. If H is living on such a tight budget every month, I am not persuaded that it is reasonable for him to spend $550 on his motorcycle instalments and petrol every month, when he could spend considerably less on public transport.

46     In the overall analysis, I order that H pay D a sum of $500.00 per month in maintenance. This is approximately one-quarter of H’s income per month, which I consider to be a manageable proportion of H’s income. It also represents about 16% of D’s monthly expenses, which is slightly lower than H’s relative earning capacity of 18% of parties’ aggregate income. This, in my view, fairly adjusts for the reality that the smaller the wage, the smaller the proportion of disposable or spare income.

Whether to order lump sum maintenance

47     W sought for the maintenance to be paid in a lump sum.

48     I declined to order that maintenance be paid in a lump sum.

49     As the Court explained in WZF v WZG at [123], there are at least two reasons that lump sum child maintenance ought generally not to be ordered. First, it ignores the reality that the child’s expenses can vary significantly at short notice. Second, it can symbolically appear to take away the sense of agency of the parent without care and control.

50     In my view, the possible incarceration of H is not a sufficient reason to order lump sum maintenance. This is ultimately a contingency. Further, if H were to be incarcerated, he would not have been able to earn income and pay maintenance even if H were still married to W. I do not think that the payment structure should be arranged such that W essentially immunises herself against the risk of H’s incarceration, because instead of mimicking the budget that would have been available to her had she remained married, she is seeking to put herself in an even better position. At any rate, a 5-year lump sum maintenance would work out to be $30,000.00. Deducting this sum from H’s share of the matrimonial assets would mean that H would be left with little or no cash. I do not consider this to be a just and equitable outcome.

Division of matrimonial assets

Identification and valuation of matrimonial assets

Matrimonial home

51     H submits that the value of the matrimonial home is $520,000. The matrimonial home, a HDB flat, is 84m2 in area, and is located on the 5th floor. Based on HDB data, a similarly-sized unit on the 4th–6th floor in the same block was sold for $500,000 in Oct 2024 (ie, $553 per square foot). H submits the price of the matrimonial home has since increased to $575 per square foot, or $520,000 in total. W submitted that the value of the matrimonial home was $461,000, but this was revised to $500,000 in view of the transaction highlighted by H.

52     No reason was cited for the supposed increase in the valuation of the matrimonial home since the last similar transaction in October 2024. The increase in the sale price of other transacted units of a different size and floor was only about 1% between September 2024 and May 2025. What H seeks is a 4% increase in valuation. In my view, the evidence at best supports a 1% increase in valuation. Accordingly, I proceed on the basis that the value of the matrimonial home is $505,000.

P’s insurance policies

(1)   Great Eastern and Tokio Marine policies

53     H submitted that the surrender value of 3 insurance policies attributable to premiums paid during marriage ought to be regarded as matrimonial assets.

54     I am unable to agree with W’s submission that these are entirely pre-marital assets, since in each case about 38–47% of the premium payments (ie, a significant proportion) were made during the marriage itself. However, I accept W’s alternative submissions, which coincides with H’s submission, that the pro-rated value of the surrender value of the insurance policies (based on how much was paid during the marriage) should be treated as a matrimonial asset).

55     For this reason, I ascribe:

(a)     $862.82 to the value of the Great Eastern policy;

(b)     $2,950.54 to the value of the Tokio Marine Legacy Plus policy; and

(c)     $15,190.99 to the value of the Tokio Marine Retirement Life policy.

56     It might be argued in W’s favour that the premiums—which are of a fixed dollar value—are worth more if paid recently than if paid long ago. This is the simple principle of time value of money. In other words, it could be said that I ought to value W’s pre-marital premiums more than the premiums paid during the marriage, based on a discount rate. However, I accept H’s argument that there is no interest rate that is either explicit on the face of the insurance policies, or implicit from the terms of repayment. There is also no interest as to the interest rate that ought to be ascribed based on the couple’s use of money. Accordingly, I am unable to compute the discounted (or compounded) values of W’s premium payments over the years, and will simply proceed on the basis of the nominal values above.

(2)   AIA policy

57     W submitted that the AIA policy should be excluded as the beneficiary of the policy was their child. H had no further submissions, save to assert that it was acquired with funds during the marriage.

58     I agree with W. Even though the AIA policy was purchased with funds during the marriage, that alone does not make it a matrimonial asset. The policy was meant for the benefit of the child, and parties should not be required to treat this policy as a matrimonial asset which can be liquidated and realised in cash or cash equivalent, as this would mean that the child would lose the benefit of the insurance policy.

W’s car and motorbike

59     The parties agree on the value of the car and motorbike.

60     Where parties differ is the value of the outstanding loan. W had initially stated that the outstanding loans were $145,709.91 and $36,000 for the car and motorbike respectively. However, those were the outstanding loans as of April 2024. H, on the other hand, said that the outstanding loans as of the date of the AM hearing were $116,573.91 and $20,000.00 respectively, based on how much would have been repaid from then until August 2025 (being the month in which the AM hearing commenced). W concedes—and I agree—that the principle is correct, ie, these items should be valued as at the date of the AM hearing. However, I do not accept the parties’ computation as to the present value of the loan. H’s computation assumes that the entire monthly repayment goes towards the principal of the loan—an approach which is patently at odds with the structure of the loan. Both parties have also assumed, somewhat erroneously, a value of the loan based on the sum total of outstanding payments to be made.

61     Given that I have disagreed with both parties’ computations, some explanation as to my view of the misstep seems necessary.

62     Based on the original loan documents, the purchase price of the car was $214,898.00 inclusive of the Certificate of Entitlement. To this, a “term charge” of $24,908.80 was added, making the total amount payable in respect of the finance arrangement $239,806.00. Of this sum, $86,898.00 was paid in cash, and the balance payable was stated to be $152,908.80. The effective interest rate was said to be 5.19% per annum (this, I find, was rounded off from about 5.18774% per annum). The principal sum borrowed would have been $128,000.00, being $214,898.00 less $86,898.00 (and the sum of $24,908.80 would have been the sum total of interest payments over the lifetime of the loan). Based on these parameters, the outstanding principal component of the loan as at April 2024 should have been $125,459.24. It appears to me that W could only have computed a value of the outstanding loan that was higher than the total principal borrowed, by including the interest payments within the sum of the outstanding loan. H’s computation, it appears to me, is simply W’s computation less $1,821.00 × 16 months, being the sum total of repayments.

63     However, in computing the value of the assets, the court is concerned with the principal, and not the value of interest that may be charged over the remaining lifetime of the loan.

64     Based on the parameters, the outstanding principal on the car loan as at 24 August 2025 would have been $102,954.35.

65     By a similar computation, the outstanding principal on the motorbike loan as at 13 August 2025 would have been $29,042.23.

66     To be sure, it has not escaped my notice that the agreed value of the car and motorbike—$258,888 and $45,000—are higher than the original purchase price of $214,898 and $40,000, even though motor vehicles are with few exceptions thought to depreciate rather than appreciate with time. It might have been the case that these values had somehow taken into account the interest component in the loan. However, insofar as this is the case, I have been unable to make any other finding as to the value of the car and motorbike given that these values were agreed between parties and there does not appear to be any further documentary evidence on the market value of these vehicles. I have therefore taken these sums at face value.

67     In the circumstances, I find that the value of the car is $258,888.00 - $102,954.35 = $155,933.65, and the value of the car is $45,000.00 - $29,042.23 = $15,957.77.

W’s Companies

68     W had shares in 5 companies. The valuation of these companies has been a highly contested issue. While H accepted that one company had no value, H had engaged a valuer to assess the value of three companies, and H sought to rely on the valuations of two of these. For the third, as well as another company, H urged the Court to draw an adverse inference against W for concealment of information, and to give a 10% uplift to the final ratio of division. On the other hand, W sought to have the Court distribute the shares in kind, as a way to overcome the vagaries of valuation, on the authority of Chan Seng Onn SJ’s decision in CYH v CYI [2023] SGHCF 4 (“CYH”).

69     As a preliminary matter, therefore, I address why I have not been persuaded that I must take the approach in CYH.

70     To begin, I do not read CYH as laying down a rule of law that shares should be divided in kind in lieu of valuation, where the valuation is greatly disputed. Rather, CYH simply makes it clear that this is one option in the court’s toolbox in effecting a just and equitable division of assets.

71     Next, and respectfully, I do not think CYH overcomes the problem of the vagaries of valuation, because the ratio pursuant to which the shares are distributed will be arbitrary if the court abstains from taking a view as to the value of the shares, and there is no guarantee that the “margin of error” in the distribution of shares in kind will be any less than that of ascribing a value to the shares. In CYH, the Court ordered the husband to give his wife 40% of the shares the value of which was disputed, on the basis of a broad-brush ratio of 60:40 in favour of the husband (at [20]). However, it is not clear how the court arrived at the ratio in the first place, if the value of the shares were not found.

72     Accordingly, I will proceed to express a view on the value of the companies to the extent possible. It is only in the case of one company—[M] Pte Ltd—where the value is disputed, that I will as a last resort order the division of the shares in kind. I have done this as it appears to me that, even in respect of the other companies, there is no way for W to satisfy the division of assets without alienating her shareholding in her other companies.

(1)   [J] Pte Ltd

73     There is effectively no dispute that the value of this company is 0.

(2)   [K] Pte Ltd; [L] Pte Ltd

74     These 2 companies were valued by a valuer appointed by H, to be $1,124,699.00 and $872,869.00, respectively. W did not produce any third-party valuation. Accordingly, I find the values posited by H to be more reliable and proceed on that basis.

(3)   [M] Sdn Bhd

75     W claimed that [M] Sdn Bhd did not start running any business as it was too expensive to do so, and that it was not in operation and there were no employees.

76     H disputes the value of [M] Sdn Bhd, and submits that an adverse inference be drawn in relation to it, on the basis that there is a prima facie case of concealment by W as to [M] Sdn Bhd’s value. H points out that:

(a)     [M] Sdn Bhd has committed to a non-cancellable operating lease in the sum of some MYR 240,000;

(b)     [M] Sdn Bhd collected MYR 110,352 in revenue, and there was MYR 110,352 in sales to an entity controlled by key management personnel.

(c)     [M] Sdn Bhd disbursed MYR 29,408 to W qua director, even though W claims that she has not received remuneration from [M] Sdn Bhd;

(d)     [M] Sdn Bhd has a positive equity and liabilities in the sum of some MYR 106,600, such that there is a substratum of evidence that [M] Sdn Bhd is a going concern.

77     I am not persuaded by H’s arguments. Briefly, much of H’s arguments stem, in my view, from a misreading of the financial statements. I observe that:

(a)     The financial statements are audited, ie, they have been certified to give a true and fair view of company’s financials. This makes the financial statements reliable, and weakens H’s case of concealment.

(b)     The notes disclose that there is a material uncertainty as to whether [M] Sdn Bhd can continue operating as a going concern, given the net capital deficiency. It is meaningless to point out that [M] Sdn Bhd has positive equity and liabilities. The real issue is whether it has equity—in this case, it has negative equity; it has accumulated losses and is therefore strictly speaking insolvent.

(c)     It is not clear to me that [M] Sdn Bhd has commenced business in the sense usually understood. It appears to me that the MYR 110,352 in revenue—an oddly specific sum—was in fact the same sale that was made to an entity controlled by key management personnel and disclosed as a related party transaction.

(d)     It is patently untrue that [M] Sdn Bhd disbursed MYR 29,408 to W. Instead, the notes disclose that this sum was an unsecured, interest-free and repayable-on-demand loan from W to [M] Sdn Bhd. The fact such a loan was made suggests that [M] Sdn Bhd is under financial strain.

78     While [M] Sdn Bhd has a non-cancellable operating lease, I am prepared to accept that it has committed to some preparatory steps to carry on business, before taking the view that business was not commercially viable. In the circumstances, the proposition that [M] Sdn Bhd has a positive net value is in my view too uncertain. I consider that it has zero value and decline to draw an adverse inference against W in this regard.

(4)   [M] Pte Ltd

79     In respect of [M] Pte Ltd, the valuer ascribed a value of nil to this company. However, the valuer pointed out that this value should be “interpreted with caution in view of the information constraints noted in this report”. Specifically, on the one hand, the company has a negative book value of about $895,000 (and, in fact, has reported significant losses for 3 consecutive years), which raises a concern as to its solvency and long-term viability. On the other hand, the valuer noted that management appears to operate the entity as a going concern, and management also declined to provide bank statements requested with a view to reconciling financial data and determining if the company could be treated as a going concern.

80     As alluded to above, while there may be some concealment in this case, I consider that, given the mechanics of the remainder of the division, it is preferable to divide these shares in kind to overcome the issue of vagaries in the valuation process.

Alleged dissipations by W

81     H sought to notionally reinstate some $978,000 to the matrimonial pool, being amounts which H says W dissipated from her bank account. For the purpose of submissions, this was divided into 6 categories. However, I restore only $28,000 to the matrimonial pool.

(1)   FAST/PayNow transactions in Dec 2023 totalling $259,936.30

82     Under this category, H highlighted 3 offending transactions where W transferred out $90,160.30, $115,976 and $53,800 from her account on 3 days. W, in her interrogatories, claimed that she recalls that these sums were used to buy gold on behalf of a friend who had transferred her the money to do so.

83     I accept W’s explanations, even though H has highlighted the paucity of documentary evidence. In my view, the bank statements are largely self-explanatory. On those 3 days, and before W made the payments, sums of equal or larger amounts had come in. The sum total of deposits on those 3 days was over $270,000, and at no time was W operating at a deficit on account of these transfers. W, it appears, has conveniently ignored the abnormally large inflows and focused only on the outflows.

84     In the circumstances, I do not find that this amounts to dissipation.

(2)   FAST/PayNow transactions in Jul 2022 for $9,000.00; FAST/PayNow transactions which P cannot recall, totalling $496,141.71

85     H highlighted 1 offending withdrawal of $9,000 dated 20 July 2022, which W says was among several payments intended for parties’ trip to Finland in December 2022. H also says that W claimed that she was unable to recall the details of FAST/PayNow transfers out of her account between Jul 2022 and Dec 2023, totalling $496,141.71.

86     In support of these arguments, H says that divorce proceedings were imminent even though they were commenced in October 2023, because as early as July 2022 W had contemplated divorce and had made H sign a “Marriage Agreement” which H pointed out provided as follows:

Without fulfilment to any of the above, [W] will have the rights to ask for uncontested divorce with the above agreement. No monies or shares will be given from the company. Non negotiable. Alimony at $2000. All asset will belong to [W] and [D] including the house

87     H also argued that W had, pursuant to her allegation of family violence against H in March 2023, took H’s phone, took and sold H’s motorcycle, took H’s passbook, and withdrew $13,000 from H’s account purportedly to return the monies to [M] Pte Ltd. On this basis, H argues that W must have contemplated divorce from Mar 2023, if not from Jul 2022.

88     However, H has unfairly characterised the July 2022 agreement. That agreement contained 17 specific demands that W made of H in terms of his conduct, and provided that, “Within this 3 months, there is no change, no desired communication, you will sign on the divorce agreement letter”. In my view, this is more fairly characterised as an instance where a wife was giving a wayward husband a last chance at salvaging the marriage. I note that, in December 2022 and February 2023—which is more than 3 months after the agreement, the family went holidaying in Europe and North Asia. On this basis, I do not regard that divorce was imminent as of July 2022. To be clear, neither do I regard this as a case W acted under the impression that divorce was imminent such that the power to restore dissipated assets to the pool ought to be exercised nonetheless.

89     I am also not entirely persuaded that W’s conduct in March meant that divorce was imminent. As I observe, parties had just returned from holiday, and divorce proceedings would not be filed until 7 months later.

90     In my view, in the absence of a further milestone between March 2023 (the alleged family violence) and October 2023 (the filing of the divorce), I consider that a reasonable cut-off date is 13 July 2023, being 3 months prior to the filing of the divorce. This appears to be a suitable cut-off in the circumstances because W had offered explanations for all the offending transactions in June and, as it turns out, I do not think the offending transactions in July 2023 ought to be regarded as substantial dissipations.

91     As for the $9,000.00 allegedly spent on the trip to Europe, I do not accept that this sum is to be regarded as dissipated and returnable to the matrimonial pool.

92     In the list of other offending transactions, H had listed transactions with a minimum threshold of $1,000.00. In determining the substantiality of an expense in connection with dissipation, Teh Hwee Hwee J in XIK v XIL [2025] SGHCF 16 at [28] highlighted 3 relevant factors:

(a)     The quantum of the impugned expenditure, evaluated in connection with its nature and purpose;

(b)     The quantum of the sums of similar spending (if any) before and during the relevant period; and

(c)     The aggregation of similar expenses within the relevant period, if the aggregation more accurately reflects the true nature and extent of the impugned spending (such that, on the facts, it might be that multiple similar purchases close in time might be in substance the same as a single combined purchase).

93     The quantum of the impugned expenditure itself must take bearing from the parties’ financial circumstances. A hundred-dollar expenditure may be significant for a family struggling to make ends meet, whereas a thousand-dollar expenditure may be par for the course for a well-to-do family. In this case, considering that W’s declared income is in the region of $8,000–$9,000 per month, I would consider that W ought to have some recollection for expenditures which are at least half her monthly income (in this case, I will take the cut-off as $4,500, being a round sum), and that where she denies recalling what the transaction is, it would not suffice to baldly assert that she had checked with the bank and was unable to obtain records of the payor for those transactions. I also consider that transfers within ± 1 calendar day may be aggregated for the purpose of determining if the aggregated transactions exceed the threshold for transfers. However, I have excluded transactions which are accompanied by an abnormally large inflow of a comparable amount within ±1 day of the outflow.

94     Applying these criteria, I find that, between August and December 2023, there is only one transaction which calls for an explanation—a withdrawal of $4,875 on 22 Aug 2023, which was accompanied by a deposit of $2,255 on the same day (leaving a shortfall of some $2,600). However, even this difference has been more than accounted for by the excess deposits across the other transactions in issue. In the final analysis, I consider that everything sufficiently material has been accounted for:

95     Accordingly, I do not restore any amount to the matrimonial pool on account of these transactions.

(3)   FAST/PayNow transactions from Jan 2024 to Mar 2024, totalling $50,532.00

96     Next, H takes issue with the sum of $50,532.00, which W withdrew from her bank account, citing the need to pump fresh funds into [M] Pte Ltd to pay for employee salaries or to transfer payments received from customers of [M] Pte Ltd on its behalf.

97     Of this sum, H has only identified 3 offending transactions the sum of which totals $40,532.00. $20,532 were said to be payments received on behalf of [M] Pte Ltd. There is some evidence to support this suggestion, considering that W had received a deposit of $40,000.00 on the same day before this withdrawal was made. This would not have been money belonging to W and I decline to restore this to the matrimonial pool.

98     On the other hand, the $20,000.00 which was withdrawn to pay for employees’ salaries is strictly speaking a large capital injection made after divorce proceedings commenced. I do not regard this as a transaction conducted in the ordinary course of living or business, and W did not produce any evidence to suggest that H had impliedly consented to this. I therefore restore the sum of $20,000.00 to the matrimonial pool.

(4)   AMEX withdrawals/bill payments in totalling $156,777.00

99     H points out that, between Jul 2022 and Mar 2024, W made withdrawals or bill payments in respect of her Amex account of $262,785, plus bill payments of $65,289. H argues that the difference between W’s income and the total withdrawals/payments should be added back into the pool, ie, ($262,785 + $65,289) – (21 months × $8,157/month) = $156,777.

100    In my view, most of H’s arguments do not pass muster. In XHG v XHH [2025] SGHCF 2 at [38], Choo Han Teck J declined to uplift the husband’s share of the matrimonial assets when he argued that the wife had spent lavishly after the marriage broke down. Choo J held that since the husband had access to the wife’s bank statements and credit card statements, he should have taken issue with individual expenses. Likewise, I consider that H should have taken issue with specific expenses here, rather than say that her spending in aggregate is too lavish. Annex A of H’s submissions contained a list of offending transactions, but those relating to Amex were listed as bills rather than individual expenses. For this reason, I generally decline to restore the impugned amounts to the matrimonial pool. Taking H’s case at its highest, I will consider specific expenses raised in H’s submissions. Of these, I will not take cognisance of those dated in March–April 2023, as these were not transactions when divorce was imminent. There were 3 transactions dated 12–13 Dec 2023 which total about $8,240, made to either Lazada or Ali*Taobao (which appear to be online shopping). As the quantum of these transactions appear large, and W offered no explanation even though they postdated the filing of these proceedings and therefore ought to be in W’s memory, I consider these to be dissipations, and I add back a round sum of $8,000 to the matrimonial pool.

(5)   Funds transfer/D2P in the sum of $6,000.00

101    H argues that the total withdrawals for fund transfers and D2P—$5,000 and $1,000 in total respectively, were unexplained and therefore should be restored to the matrimonial pool.

102    It appears to me that the offending D2P transaction was dated Nov 2022, while the offending funds transfers were dated Oct 2022–Jan 2023. These transactions did not take place when the divorce was imminent.

H’s motorcycle

103    W and H gave values of $2,329 and $2,193 for the motorcycle. These figures are, in my judgment, reasonable and not far apart. I therefore proceed on the basis that its value is $2,261, being the midpoint of these two figures.

H’s bank account

104    W says H’s POSB account, in which there is a credit of $26,826.27, should be counted as a matrimonial asset. H, on the other hand, claims that these moneys are pre-matrimonial assets and are therefore to be excluded from the matrimonial pool.

105    In my view, this amount must be included in the matrimonial pool because there have been several deposits and withdrawals from this account, such that any pre-matrimonial moneys would have been irretrievably commingled with matrimonial funds.

Chattels

106    H seeks to include a number of chattels, such as:

(a)     A Martell bottle containing loose change, which H values at $3,000;

(b)     A GMT Master II Rolex watch, which H values at $42,500 ;

(c)     Gold bars (~30g), which H values at $3,000;

(d)     Liquor, including a Martell Cordon Bleu and VSOP; which H values at $3,000;

(e)     a Seiko Limited Edition Watch, which H values at $400;

(f)     3 Louis Vuitton belts, which H values at $3,000;

(g)     1 Louis Vuitton wallet, which H values at $1,000; and

(h)     2 Coach Bags, which H values at $1,000.

107    I decline to include these items in the matrimonial pool, as I consider the evidence too unreliable for me to act upon.

108    Although H somewhat makes an implicit claim that he owns a GMT Master II Rolex watch which was left at the matrimonial home when W chased H out of it, this has not been demonstrated to me on a balance of probabilities. H tendered a picture showing W wearing what was said to be that watch, but W disputed it, claiming that that watch was hers and bought with her own money. There were also no documents showing what a GMT Master II Rolex watch would look like, when it was bought, or either how much it cost or how much it could be sold for. In the premises, it is unsafe for me to conclude that this watch, if it even exists, should be part of the matrimonial pool.

109    W has essentially denied that any of these items remain at the matrimonial home, as H had collected all his belongings. Even if it is true, as H alleges, that he had left the items in the matrimonial home and was unable to retrieve them because W had barred him from entering, H has not been able to provide any documentary proof of purchase/ownership or even value; this H should have been able to do without access to the physical items.

110    I now address the only (apparent) exception—the liquor. H produced a receipt which shows the purchase of a “Chivas Regal 25 YO” for £188, and a “Royal Salute 21YO 70” for £99, among other items. However, the simple fact of the matter is that the receipt was dated 11 Sep 2017, which is before the parties were married. If this liquor, as H appears to try to have me believe, was not consumed, then it is unclear how this has been transformed into a matrimonial asset. In any event, neither of these bottles were particularised in the list of assets tendered by H. In my view, it does not suffice to identify chattels by a mere reference to their class, especially if they are not fungible and are thought to be individually material to the overall pool of assets. The only bottles of liquor which were particularised were the Martell Cordon Bleu and the VSOP.

Conclusion

111    In summary, the matrimonial pool comprises the following:

Contributions

Direct financial contributions

(1)   To the matrimonial home

112    In terms of each party’s contributions to the matrimonial home, P’s contributions through her CPF account and D’s contributions to the mortgage loan were not contested. There were three main areas of dispute.

113    First, each party did not appear to admit the other party’s contribution to the renovation expenses. However, neither party offered persuasive evidence to refute the other party’s alleged contributions. I accept each party’s evidence as to their own contributions, as it appears that the scope of work that each party paid for does not appear to be mutually exclusive.

114    Second, I accept H’s evidence as to his initial capital repayment as he has given evidence which does not appear inherently incredible, and W has not shown any documentary evidence that contradicts H’s claims.

115    Third, H claims that $35,000 of W’s CPF contributions are attributable to him, being half the housing grant of $70,000 received by W. H says that his presence was necessary to form the family nucleus to make W eligible to receive the grant. I accept H’s argument. W has not demonstrated that she would have received the grant without H’s presence. Accordingly, I find that the grant should be attributable to both parties in equal shares.

(2)   To the companies

116    H submits that the companies were incorporated during the marriage with matrimonial moneys, and that the shares should therefore be equally attributed to both parties as their respective direct contributions.

117    W did not quite address this submission directly, but it does not appear that W seriously contends that the companies were incorporated using funds set aside from before the marriage. Given, also, the fact that W has now submitted that the shares of the company should be distributed in kind to H in the ratio of the parties’ contributions to the marriage, I am prepared to accept H’s submission broadly.

118    However, H has in my view made two missteps in his analysis:

(a)     First, the quantum of the contribution should not be the value of the company, but its paid-up capital. The difference is properly the retained earnings of the company, which are the profits of the marriage to be distributed in the division but which are not reckoned as either party’s direct financial contribution.

(b)     Second, each party should not be taken to have made equal contribution to the company by virtue only of the fact that the company was incorporated using parties’ joint capital. The parties’ relative financial contributions to the marriage must be assessed in the first place. In principle, each party should be taken to have contributed in proportion to their financial capacity at the time of the incorporation of the companies. Based on the parties’ notices of assessment for YA2023, W earned about twice H’s income in 2022, the year [L] Pte Ltd was incorporated. The parties’ income for 2019 and 2020—the years that [M] Pte Ltd and [K] Pte Ltd were incorporated—is unclear. While W’s income for YA2022 was lower than H’s income, I do not regard that as a necessarily reliable proxy for the parties’ respective financial strength in 2019–2020. The parties’ relative incomes are not conclusive, either: the parties’ savings are also relevant to determining their relative financial strengths. Broadly, I consider it more likely that W brought slightly more to the marriage in terms of savings than H. I therefore consider that 60% of the paid-up capital is attributable to W.

119    Since parties each have 1,000 shares in [J] Pte Ltd, I consider them to have each contributed to the acquisition of their own shares. As for [M] Sdn Bhd, it does not appear that there is a company search indicating its paid-up capital. In the event, I will not ascribe a level of contribution to it by either party. In any event, the parties’ contributions to it can be approximated from their contributions to the paid-up capital of the other 4 companies.

(3)   Conclusion

120    In summary, my view as to parties’ contributions are as follows:

Ratio of contribution

Indirect contributions

121    W argued that indirect contributions should be 80% in her favour, while H argued that indirect contributions should be at least 50% in his favour.

122    In terms of indirect non-financial contributions, I am satisfied that both parents were generally involved in caring for D. Although H moved out of the matrimonial home in 2023, parties did not agree on the exact nature of his moving out. W sought to characterise this as a case of H leaving the home, whereas H said that W had chased him out. Considering that H left the home around the same time as W had reported him for certain sexual offences, I consider it more likely that W had effectively excluded H from the matrimonial home. For this reason, the fact that W would have, in the past 2 years, spent much more time and effort than H caring for D is of limited credit to W. For this reason, I do not consider it just and equitable to assess W’s indirect contributions at 80%.

123    Although W was named as the shareholder and director of many of the companies (but not H), I am satisfied that H also contributed towards the development of the companies, the value of which has grown significantly from its paid-up capital.

124    In terms of indirect financial contributions, I am satisfied that both parties contributed substantially to the marriage. I consider that W would have contributed somewhat more given that W generally had a markedly higher income than H. There have been some suggestions that W spent lavishly on luxury goods for herself. However, I decline to adjust the ratio of indirect contributions in favour of H in this regard because it appears from H’s own fact and position sheet that he too has had his fair share of indulgences, such as a Rolex watch and liquor collection collectively worth over $45,000.

125    In the final analysis, I consider it appropriate to ascribe W with 60% indirect contribution.

Ratio of direct and indirect contributions

126    I ascribe 50% to each of direct and indirect contributions, this being the default and there being no submissions otherwise.

Conclusion

127    I find that, overall, W and H are entitled to 64.25% and 35.75% of the matrimonial assets respectively.

128    As I have not expressed a view as to the value of [M] Pte Ltd, my findings on the ratio of division will not have taken care of the shares of [M] Pte Ltd. These are to be distributed in kind to the parties. W is to transfer 13,260 shares in [M] Pte Ltd to H.

html:
VPN v VPO

VPN v VPO
[2026] SGFC 29

Case Number:MSS 1019 of 2024
Decision Date:10 March 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Goh Zhuo Neng
Counsel Name(s): The Mother in person; The Father in person.
Parties: VPN — VPO

Enforcement of Maintenance Order – s71 Women’s Charter

10 March 2026

District Judge Goh Zhuo Neng:

A.   INTRODUCTION

1       This is an enforcement application in respect of a consent order dated 6 March 2020 which has been varied several times, most recently in 28 November 2025. Parties are divorced, with one child who the Mother has care and control of.

2       At the time the application was filed on 3 May 2024, the terms of the order for child maintenance were for the Father to pay the Mother as monthly maintenance for the child, 65% of the all the child’s expenses (except overseas holidays) within 7 days of production of receipts/bills into the Mother’s nominated account. There had also been a prior enforcement order made in MSS xxx of 2022 on 15 November 2022 which had assessed arrears of maintenance as being $3,261.58 as of 8 June 2022 (up to and including the May 2022 maintenance only).

3       On 28 November 2025, I gave my decision in several summonses that had been filed by the parties in VPN v VPO [2026] SGFC 6 (“Variation Applications”). In doing so, I also varied the child maintenance order from a reimbursement order to a monthly fixed sum payment which is backdated to June 2022 so that the arrears will be seamlessly calculated from the last enforcement order. These orders are set out below:

1.     From the period from June 2022 to February 2026, the Father will pay the Mother $350 per month as maintenance for the Child.

2.     From March 2026 onwards, the Father will pay the Mother $575 a month as maintenance for the Child.

3.     Maintenance payments for the Child will be made on the 15th Day of each month into the account designated for payment by the Mother in writing to the Father. For avoidance of doubt, the Father shall not be entitled to set off any amounts spent directly on the Child by him from these fixed sum maintenance payments to the Mother.

4       On 10 December 2025, the Father filed an appeal against my decision in the variation applications. No stay of execution was ordered pending appeal.

5       On 16 December 2025, I heard this application and made the following orders:

(a)     Arrears from June 2022 to December 2025 are $14,767.18.

(b)     These arrears will be repaid in monthly instalments on top of the monthly maintenance:

(i)       January 2026 and February 2026 - $150 a month arrears instalments and $350 monthly maintenance for a total of $500 each month.

(ii)       March 2026 onwards - $125 a month arrears instalments and $575 monthly maintenance for a total of $700 each month.

(c)     Payments to be made on the 15th day of each month into the Mother’s designated account.

6       On 30 December 2025 the Father filed an appeal against my order. I set out below my grounds of decision.

B.   THE LAW

7       The application was filed under s71 of the Women’s Charter 1961. S71(1) and (2) as of 11 October 2024, provides that:

Enforcement of maintenance order

71.-(1)     If any person fails to make one or more payments required to be made under a maintenance order, the court which made the order may do all or any of the following:

(a)     for every breach of the order by warrant direct the amount due to be levied in the manner by law provided for levying fines imposed by a Magistrate’s Court;

(b)     sentence the person to imprisonment for a term not exceeding one month for each month’s allowance remaining unpaid;

(c)     make an enforcement order for attachment of a debt in accordance with the Family Justice Rules made under section 79;

[Act 25 of 2021 wef 15/10/2024]

(d)     order the person to furnish security against any future default in maintenance payments by means of a banker’s guarantee which –

(i)     must be valid for such period (not exceeding 3 years) as the court may determine, starting from the date the order for security is made; and

(ii)    must be for an amount not exceeding 3 months of maintenance payable under the maintenance order;

(e)     if the court considers it in the interests of the parties in the maintenance proceedings or their children to do so, order the person to undergo financial counselling or such other similar or related programme as the court may direct;

(f)     make a community service order requiring the person to perform any unpaid community service for up to 40 hours under the supervision of a community service officer.

[2/2011; 27/2014; 7/2016]

(2)    A sentence of imprisonment ordered under subsection (1)(b) does not affect or diminish the obligation of the person against whom the maintenance order is made to make the payment or payments under the maintenance order which that person has failed to make, except that the court may, if it thinks fit, reduce the amount of any such payments.

8       In this regard, the applicable legal principles relating to the enforcement proceedings are well established.

9       In Lai Ching Kin v Ng Chin Chye [2001] SGDC 228, the District Court noted that the quintessential characteristic of enforcement proceedings is for a respondent to be given a chance to “show cause” (i.e. provide reasons which are satisfactory to the Court) why an enforcement order should not be made against him or her.

10     Whether a respondent had “shown cause” is a fact-sensitive exercise given the myriad of possible reasons why a person did not make payment in accordance with the terms of a maintenance order. Without being prescriptive or exhaustive, circumstances in which the Family Courts had found a respondent to have sufficiently “shown cause” include:

(a)     Where the parties had mutually agreed to a reduction of the amount payable pursuant to maintenance order or where there was an understanding (whether implicit or explicit) between the parties not to require strict compliance with the terms of the said order (see for eg., Lai Ching Kin v Ng Chin Chye [2001] SGDC 228 at [15], UAE v UAF [2017] SGFC 46 at [24] – [28] and VAM v VAN [2019] SGFC 96).

(b)     Where the respondent had went over and beyond what was required under the maintenance order and/or had provided additional support to the complainant even though the payment modality may not have been exactly in accordance with the terms of the maintenance order (see TDR v TDS [2014] SGDC 183 at [22] – [27]).

(c)     Where a complainant had used monies belonging to the respondent to pay for expenses which were within the scope of the maintenance order such that the respondent should be treated as having paid for those expenses (see for eg. TXY v TXZ [2017] SGFC 21).

(d)     Where a respondent had a good track record of payment but is facing genuine financial difficulties in keeping up with the payments (see for eg. VSP v VSQ [2021] SGFC 71, a case involving financial difficulties brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic).

11     Ultimately, the touchstone of the inquiry is whether the making of an enforcement order would lead to injustice and/or where it would be inequitable to do so, given the circumstances and facts of a given case.

C.   APPLICATION OF LAW TO THE FACTS

12     For completeness and for ease of reference during the hearing of this application, the Father agreed that the evidence in the Variation Applications would also be admitted for use in these enforcement proceedings. However, there was no new evidence presented before me during the hearing which indicated that I should change my views on the Father’s ability to pay maintenance.

I.    The Arrears

13     During the hearing, the Father also attempted to cross examine the Mother on specific items that she had been seeking reimbursement for under the original terms of the order. This was with the intention of deducting them from the monthly fixed sum maintenance I had ordered. I explained to him that this was not necessary since I had already ordered a backdated variation of the maintenance order and converted it to a fixed sum maintenance payment based on my own assessment of the child’s reasonable expenses. So we did not need to spend time evaluating the reasonableness of expenses incurred by the Mother from June 2022 to December 2025.

14     Despite this, the Father persisted with this line of cross examination and even challenged me directly – asking if I was determining that the items she was seeking reimbursement for were reasonably incurred. This argument completely missed the point I had made about the order being changed to a fixed maintenance payment. So to avoid an unnecessarily lengthy cross examination which would have contributed nothing to the assessment of the arrears, I limited his time for cross examination to 10 minutes, during which he continued to ask questions about the reasonableness of those past expenses.

15     Accordingly, I calculated the outstanding amount of arrears based on the amounts due under the previous maintenance order in MSS xxx of 2022, the amounts payable under the varied child maintenance order from June 2022 to December 2025 and deducting the amounts which the parties agreed the Father had paid since June 2022. I arrived at a total of $14,767.18 as at and including December 2025 maintenance.

Description

Amount

Remarks

Arrears as at 8 June 2022 under MSS xxx of 2022 (up till May 2022)

3,261.58

 

June 2022 to December 2025 (maintenance payable on 15th day of each month under the varied order)

15,050

2023 – 12 months

2024 – 12 months

A total of 43 months of $350

Amounts paid by the Father from June 2022 to April 2024 – 72.80 a month

-1,674.40

-1,520

-350

14,767.18

 



II.    Showing Cause – The Father’s recycling of arguments in the Variation Application

16     Here, the Father repeated the arguments in the Variation Applications, essentially that he was unable to make payment as he had no full time employment since he lost his job in March 2020 which paid him a basic salary of $7,000. I had already assessed those arguments in the Variation Applications and did not find them meritorious as I believed that he still had sufficient capacity to pay the Mother a minimum of $350 a month from June 2020 onwards. He had an earning capacity of about $5,000 and was actually earning $1,500 a month with odd jobs. He was also insisting on living in a 4-bedroom sized flat whose housing loan was being serviced by his aged Mother so that he could maintain the Child’s original residence during the marriage. There was a misplaced sense of priorities which should have been used to address the expenses of the Child.

17     As stated in the Variation Applications, I accepted that the Father was currently not engaged in full time employment. Therefore I did not expect him to make a higher level of payment at this point. At an actual income of $1,500, he should be able to pay $500 of his salary for the Child, which meant $150 in arrears and $350 in monthly maintenance. When his income eventually increased to $5,000 under his income capacity as determined in the Variation Application, he should be able to pay $700 a month, which would be $125 in arrears and $575 in monthly maintenance.

D.   CONCLUSION

18     For the foregoing reasons, I made all the above orders.

case-number: Divorce No 3679 of 2024, Registrar's Appeal No 3 of 2026
title: XZE v XZF
counsel: Remya Aravamuthan (High Street Chambers LLC) for the plaintiff
Tan Xuan Qi Dorothy and Lim Fang-Yu Mathea (Kith & Kin Law Corporation) for the defendant
html:
XZE v XZF

XZE v XZF
[2026] SGFC 45

Case Number:Divorce No 3679 of 2024, Registrar's Appeal No 3 of 2026
Decision Date:30 March 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Kenneth Yap
Counsel Name(s): Remya Aravamuthan (High Street Chambers LLC) for the plaintiff; Tan Xuan Qi Dorothy and Lim Fang-Yu Mathea (Kith & Kin Law Corporation) for the defendant
Parties: XZE — XZF

Family Law – Examination of Children – Expert Evidence – Whether leave should be granted for applicant to adduce and rely upon medical reports

30 March 2026

District Judge Kenneth Yap:

Introduction

1       This appeal is against the Assistant Registrar’s (“AR”) decision to dismiss the Mother’s application for leave under Rule 35(4) of the Family Justice Rules 2014 (“FJR 2014”) to adduce medical reports from two doctors to ascertain the special educational needs of the child.

2       I dismissed the appeal and provide my reasons as follows.

Facts

The Parties

3       The parties married on 24 November 2011, with one male child to the marriage, aged 13. They resided with the child in Australia for a majority of the marriage, until the Mother relocated with the child to Singapore in early 2024.

4       Divorce proceedings commenced on 13 August 2024, and interim judgment was granted on 17 February 2025. Parties have now agreed on all ancillary matters save for maintenance, which will in turn be impacted by the question of whether the child attends a private as opposed to government special needs school (“the disputed issue”).

Background to the Dispute

5       The medical reports in question are provided by Senior Consultant Psychiatrist Dr Jared Ng dated 22 September 2025 and Senior Consultant Dr Tian Cheong Sing dated 22 October 2025. It is not disputed that the child has been diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder with mild intellectual disability. It is also agreed between the parents that the child does require placement in a special needs school.

6       The Mother submits that these reports would assist the Court on:

(a)     The child’s appropriate educational placement;

(b)     The need for shadow support/aide in the school environment;

(c)     The level and structure of therapeutic and development interventions required; and

(d)     The management of transition-related risks.

7       The Mother adduced the report by Dr Jared Ng in her Affidavit of Assets and Means filed on 2 October 2025. The Father sought to have this medical report expunged as it was adduced without leave of Court as required under Rule 35 of the FJR 2014. In response, the Mother filed SUM 2667 of 2025 to apply for leave to adduce Dr Jared Ng’s report and in her supporting affidavit to this application, further adduced Dr Tian Cheong Sing’s report.

8       Rule 35(1) of the FJR 2014 requires parties to obtain leave of Court before causing a child to be examined by a medical practitioner for the purposes of tendering expert evidence in family proceedings. Where such leave has not been obtained, an application can be made under Rule 35(4) of the FJR 2014 to nevertheless adduce such evidence of the examination or assessment, with the leave of court. Rule 35(1) and (4) are provided as follows:

35.—(1)    Where a child is a party to or a subject of any action or proceedings, or where any action or proceedings involve the welfare or custody of a child, a party must not, without the leave of the Court, cause the child to be examined or assessed by any registered medical practitioner, psychologist, counsellor, social worker or mental health professional for the purpose of preparing expert evidence for use in those proceedings.

(4)    Where a registered medical practitioner, psychologist, counsellor, social worker or mental health professional who is not appointed by the Court pursuant to an application under paragraph (1) examines or assesses the child, no evidence arising out of the examination or assessment may be adduced without the leave of the Court.

The Medical Reports

9       Dr Jared Ng is a Senior Consultant (Psychiatrist) and Medical Director at Connections Mindhealth. He examined the child on 26 April 2024 and 3 July 2024. A further appointment on 13 December 2024 could not proceed because the child had become dysregulated. The relevant portions of Dr Jared Ng’s report are extracted as follows:

From the history provided and my observations, (the child) was a boy with significant social and emotional difficulties related to his autism and ADHD…

Given his diagnosis of autism, ADHD, and mild intellectual disability, (the child) has clear special educational needs. He requires a highly structured environment, predictable routines, and skilled adult support to manage emotional dysregulation and prevent meltdowns. A mainstream school setting without tailored support is unlikely to meet his needs. He would benefit from placement in a private special needs school with small class sizes, an autism focused curriculum, and access to therapies such as speech and occupational therapy. A one-to-one shadow teacher or classroom aide would also be helpful in supporting him during transitions and stressful settings, providing consistency and helping him apply coping strategies.

…

In summary, (the child) is a child with complex psychiatric and developmental needs … He will benefit from a comprehensive, multidisciplinary approach including psychiatric follow up, behavioural support and a school placement designed for children with autism and intellectual disability. He requires structured support at school and at home, shadow support in the classroom, and ongoing involvement of his mother, to whom he has shown a strong attachment.

[Emphasis added]

10     Dr Tian Cheong Sing is a Senior Consultant from the Department of Psychological Medicine at the National University Hospital. The Mother had brought the child to see him on 5 November 2024 following their return to Singapore in April 2024 to continue the management of the child’s Autism Spectrum Disorder. The relevant portions of his report are extracted below:

9.    In summary, (the child) suffers from an Autism Spectrum Disorder. An assessment of his cognitive functioning in Year 2018 revealed borderline intellectual functioning at that time.

10.    There are severe social communication and language difficulties and very poor emotional functioning with emotional dysregulation.
