XPX v XPW

XPX v XPW
[2026] SGFC 30

Case Number:SSP 442 of 2025
Decision Date:06 March 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Kow Keng Siong
Counsel Name(s): Ms Ganga d/o Avatiar, Ms Eileen Yeo & Ms Michelle Lynn Fernandez (M/s Advocatus Law LLP) for the Mother; Ms Sofia Bakhash (M/s Phoenix Law Corporation) for the Father.
Parties: XPX — XPW

Family Law – Family violence – Protection orders – Impermissible for application for protection orders to be used to undermine or as a backdoor to vary existing access orders

Family Law – Family violence – Sexual abuse – Whether skin-to-skin rubbing of a child’s genital area amounted to sexual abuse – Section 58B(1) and s 58B(3) Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Emotional or psychological abuse – Whether conduct causing fear, panic and distress to child during access amounted to emotional or psychological abuse – Section 58B(4)(a) and s 58B(4)(b) Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Physical abuse – Wrongful restraint – Whether parent may use force or coercion to compel resisting child to accompany him during access – Distinction between lawful parental control and coercive physical restraint – Section 58B(2)(b) Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Protection orders – Necessity – Relevance of gravity of violence, pattern of coercive conduct and respondent’s denial of harm

Family Law – Family violence – Protection orders – Whether counselling order is appropriate – Applicable considerations – Section 60E Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Protection orders – Whether stay-away order is appropriate – Applicable considerations – Section 60B(2)(b) Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Protection orders – Whether no-contact order is appropriate – Applicable considerations – Section 60B(2)(c) Women’s Charter 1961

Family Law – Family violence – Mandatory treatment order – Preliminary assessment report – Whether appropriate to call for preliminary assessment report – Applicable considerations – Section 60F(6) Women’s Charter 1961

Evidence – Weight – Whether police or child protection authorities’ decision to take no further action determinative in family violence proceedings

Words and Phrases – “wrongfully restraining a person against the person’s will” – Section 58B(2)(b) Women’s Charter 1961

6 March 2026

District Judge Kow Keng Siong:

Introduction

1       This is an application by the mother (“Mother”) under s 60A of the Women’s Charter 1961 (“Charter”) for a protection order in respect of her daughter (“Daughter”). [note: 1] The respondent is the child’s father (“Father”). The parties are divorced and they also have a son (“Son”).[note: 2]

2       To succeed, the Mother must prove two matters on a balance of probabilities:

(a)     That the Father had committed “family violence” against the Daughter.[note: 3] “Family violence” means “physical, sexual, and emotional or psychological abuse against a family member”: s 58B(1). (Issue 1)

(b)     That a protection order is “necessary for the protection or personal safety” of the child: s 60A(1). (Issue 2)

3       I note that the Father has an order granting him unsupervised access to the Daughter. He contends that the Mother is using these proceedings to frustrate his right of access. On this, I make two observations.

(a)     A court will not permit a protection order to be weaponised. In other words, it will not be allowed, among others, to serve the ulterior motive of undermining or varying (by the backdoor) an access order.

(b)     That said, access must always be exercised in accordance with the law, including the Charter’s provisions against family violence. If the relevant statutory requirements are satisfied, a protection order will be granted. It bears noting that such an order merely restrains family violence. It does not, without more, extinguish access that is exercised lawfully and without abuse.

4       I now turn to the merits of the Mother’s application.

Issue 1 – Had family violence been committed?

Basis for the application

5       The Mother relies on the following conduct by the Father to support her application for a protection order.[note: 4]

 

Date

Father’s conduct

(1)

12.10.19

Touched the Daughter’s genital area at a swimming pool. (“Swimming Pool Incident”)

(2)

02.01.22

Forcibly carried away the Daughter and tried to put her in his car against her wishes. (“Plaza Singapura Incident”)

(3)

16.01.22

Touched the Daughter in a manner that caused her discomfort. (“First Paragon Mall Incident”)

(4)

20.02.22

Wrongfully confined the Daughter. (“Second Paragon Mall Incident”)

(5)

13.03.22

Followed the Daughter despite the child telling him not to do so. (“First Bedok Mall Incident”)

(6)

20.03.22

Used his body to prevent the Daughter from leaving a restaurant. (“Second Bedok Mall Incident”)



12 October 2019 – Swimming Pool Incident

6       I begin with the Swimming Pool Incident.

Undisputed facts

7       It is undisputed that on 12 October 2019, the Father had supervised access to the children and brought them to his condominium swimming pool. [note: 5] At the material time, the Daughter was five years old. She was wearing a one-piece swimsuit with a long zip at the back.

The Daughter’s evidence

8       The Daughter’s evidence is that, at one point, the Son swam to the deeper end of the pool. As she wanted to follow him, the Father helped her swim towards her brother. He held her with one arm under her body to keep her head above water. With his other hand, he unzipped her swimsuit and rubbed her “pee pee” (genital area) with his finger (“sexual act”). She also felt something rough, like a fingernail, against her genital area. She did not know how to react and tried to focus on swimming.[note: 6]

9       The next morning, the Daughter felt pain while urinating. When she told her maternal grandmother (“Grandmother”) about it, the latter asked what had happened. The child then said that it was because the Father had touched her “pee pee”. The Grandmother called the Mother, who then questioned the child. Again, the child repeated that the Father had committed the sexual act.[note: 7]

10     The Mother was at a loss. Nonetheless, she decided to bring the Daughter out went for lunch. It was to celebrate the child’s birthday with some friends and “C” – who is the child’s godmother and the Mother’s close friend.[note: 8]

11     After lunch, the Daughter asked to use the toilet. C offered to accompany her. At the toilet, C went into the cubicle to assist the child. While urinating, the child said that her “pee pee” was painful. When asked why, she pointed to her genital area and whispered to C that the Father had committed the sexual act. According to C, the child appeared visibly uncomfortable.[note: 9]

12     C informed the Mother of what the Daughter had said. The Mother then brought the child to KK Women’s and Children’s Hospital (“KKH”).

The Father’s case

13     The Father denies committing the sexual act. He claims that (a) the child had fabricated the allegation,[note: 10] (b) someone else had committed the act,[note: 11] and (c) the Mother had coached the child to make a false accusation against him.[note: 12]

14     In the alternative, the Father contends that any touching (if it had occurred) was accidental.[note: 13]

15     Finally, the Father asserts that he has already been cleared of any wrongdoing because the Police and Child Protection Services (“CPS”) had investigated the matter and decided to take no further action against him.[note: 14] Further, a court had rejected the child’s claim when it dismissed the Mother’s application for a personal protection order in SS 1778/2019 (“SS 1778”).

Preliminary observations

16     I make two preliminary observations.

17     First, I find that the Father’s contention that he has already been exonerated is misconceived, and that whether he had committed the sexual act is very much a live issue. Let me explain.

(a)     The fact that the Police and CPS had decided to take no further action is not determinative of whether the sexual act has been disproved in these proceedings.

(i)       No evidence has been adduced as to why the authorities decided to take no further action. In the absence of such evidence, it is pure speculation to treat that decision as an investigative finding that the Daughter’s claim was false. This is because a decision to take no further action may turn on a range of considerations – including (1) whether the evidence is sufficient to prove, on the criminal standard, that the Father had committed an offence and (2) internal considerations relating to the initiation of prosecutorial and administrative actions.

(ii)       These considerations differ fundamentally from the task before me, which is simply to determine whether the Mother has proved that the sexual act amounts to family violence on a balance of probabilities: ACZ v ADC [2009] SGDC 457 at [22]; Kang May Teng, Maria Olivia v Chua Jun Yang [2025] SGDC 130 at [103].

(iii)       Further, courts have emphasised that a police warning or opinion does not amount to a legally binding pronouncement of guilt or a judicial finding of fact. Only a court can make such findings: Wham Kwok Han Jolovan v Attorney-General [2015] SGHC 324 at [34]. By parity of reasoning, a decision to take no further action is not a binding judicial finding that the Father did not commit the sexual act.

(b)     Regarding SS 1778, the Swimming Pool Incident (which occurred on 12 October 2019) is not the subject of a complaint in that case. SS 1778 was filed in August 2019 – before the Swimming Pool Incident – and relates to a different incident. The court in SS 1778 did not make any finding on whether the Father had committed the sexual act. This is plain from the following notes of evidence in that case:

In respect of the PPO application for the daughter – on the totality of the evidence adduced before it, this Court is also not satisfied that the Complainant has proven on a balance of probabilities that the Respondent (father) has committed family violence against her during his access on 2 April 2019. Therefore, the application for this child is also dismissed.[note: 15]

18     I turn to my second observation. It concerns whether the alleged sexual act amounts to “sexual abuse”.

(a)     Section 58B(3) provides that sexual abuse “includes conduct or behaviour that coerces, or attempts to coerce, a person to engage in sexual activity” (emphasis added). The use of “includes” is significant. It extends the meaning of sexual abuse to also cover conduct that puts pressure on a family member to engage in a sexual act. Section 58B(3) makes it clear that sexual abuse can occur even without physical sexual contact with the family member sought to be protected.

(b)     Nothing in either s 58B(3) or the second reading speeches suggests that conduct which, in ordinary language, amounts to sexual abuse is excluded from the concept of “family violence” or a protection order. In this regard, the ordinary meaning of “sexual abuse” would cover conduct such as sexual touching, molestation, fondling of intimate parts, sexual penetration, or other contact/violation of the body in a sexual manner.

(c)     In the present case, if the sexual act had occurred, then it would clearly amount to family violence. The Father does not seriously dispute that intentionally rubbing a child’s genital area – without lawful justification (such as medical necessity) or informed consent – is a grave violation of the child’s bodily integrity and amounts to “sexual abuse” within s 58B(1).

Key issue for determination

19     The key issue regarding the Swimming Pool Incident is thus a factual one: Did the Father commit the sexual act?

The Father’s submissions

20     To support his case that he did not commit the act, the Father relies on the fact that no injury was found on the Daughter’s genital area. I do not regard this as exculpatory. First, it is unclear what visible injury, if any, the sexual act would necessarily have produced. Second, the medical examination took place only three days after the incident, not immediately.[note: 16] Dr N – who examined the child – testified that any injury could have healed by the time of the examination.[note: 17]

21     The Father also submits that the Mother did not report the matter immediately, but only after the birthday lunch.[note: 18] I am not persuaded that this undermines the credibility of the Daughter’s claim against him.

(a)     The Mother explained that she proceeded with the lunch because the invitations had already been issued, the child wanted it to go ahead, and she did not want to disappoint the child. I find this explanation to be plausible.

(b)     In any event, there was no undue delay. Soon after the lunch, the Mother brought the child to KKH. This was just one day after the Swimming Pool Incident.

22     I also reject the Father’s submission that the Mother’s decision to apply for a protection order some 5½ years after the incident undermines the Daughter’s claim.[note: 19] I accept the following explanations by the Mother for not reporting the matter sooner.[note: 20]

(a)      First, she was busy at the material time as she was embroiled in extensive litigation with the Father. It is undisputed that between 2020 and 2022, there were 12 applications and one appeal between the parties. She had to juggle these court proceedings with caring for her children as a single mother.

(b)      Second, the Mother was in financial difficulty at the material time. She was unemployed. An application for a protection order would have placed further financial strain on her limited resources.

(c)      Finally, the Mother believed at the material time that the relevant social agencies would be able to ensure that the Daughter was protected. That belief was shattered in November 2024 when the Father was granted six hours of unsupervised access every alternate weekend. It was only then that she realised that she had to take further steps to protect the child. This prompted her to file the present application.

Finding – The Father had committed the sexual act

23     Having considered the evidence, I find on a balance of probabilities that the Father had committed the sexual act. My reasons are as follows.

Daughter did not fabricate her evidence

(a)      Clear and consistent evidence. I find the Daughter’s evidence about the sexual act to be clear, consistent, and unembellished. Where she could not remember peripheral details, she said so readily.[note: 21] The Father points to her inability to recall certain details, such as how long she remained in the pool afterwards. I do not regard this as undermining her reliability. Witnesses often forget peripheral details. The gaps in memory are not surprising, given that the incident occurred some six years before the hearing. What matters is that the Daughter is able to consistently recall the material details of the incident: GII v Public Prosecutor [2025] 3 SLR 578 at [48].

(b)      No evidence of motive to lie. At the time of the incident, the Daughter was only five years old. There is no convincing explanation for how a child of that age could have produced the account she gave if it were fabricated.

(c)      Disclosure made innocently. Further, the Daughter disclosed the sexual act just one day after it was committed. She had complained of pain during urination. It was only upon the Grandmother’s questioning that she revealed that the Father had touched her.[note: 22] The spontaneous and innocent circumstances in which the disclosure was made negates any suggestion that it was staged.

No evidence that someone else had committed the sexual act

(d)     The Father offers no evidence for his assertion that someone else had committed the act. Bare assertions do not rebut proved facts.

No evidence that Daughter had been coached

(e)     There is likewise no evidence that the Daughter was coached to falsely accuse the Father.[note: 23] I am mindful that the initial disclosure emerged after questioning by the Grandmother, and that disclosures obtained through questioning are not automatically immune from suggestion. However, on the evidence before me, there is no basis to conclude that the allegation was the product of suggestion by the Grandmother or the Mother.

Father’s denial is unpersuasive

(f)     In contrast to the Daughter’s clear and consistent evidence,[note: 24] the Father’s evidence is marked by material evidential and logical gaps. For instance, despite knowing that the commission of the sexual act lay at the heart of the Mother’s application, the Father did not provide evidence to rebut it in his affidavit.[note: 25] It was only in cross-examination that he advanced such evidence for the first time.

(g)     The Father had failed to provide a satisfactory explanation for omitting his account of the Swimming Pool Incident from his affidavit.

(i)       Initially, he claimed that he was tired of rehashing his account because he had already given it to the Police and CPS.[note: 26] I cannot accept this claim. What he may have told those authorities was for purposes unconnected with the issue before this court, namely whether a protection order should be made. Moreover, this court could not be expected to know what he had told the authorities some six years earlier.

(ii)       The Father later gave a materially different explanation, namely that he was concerned he might be “found at fault” if he recalled the details wrongly.[note: 27] I find this explanation to be unbelievable. On his own evidence, he was allegedly tired of rehashing his account. Given this, he would have remembered the Swimming Pool Incident clearly. It is odd why he would be so concerned about remembering the details wrongly.

(iii)       Additionally, I find that the Father was evasive in cross-examination, even on straightforward matters.[note: 28] This further undermines the weight that I can attach to his denial of the sexual act.

Finding – The sexual act was not accidental

24     Next, I turn to the Father’s alternative case – i.e., the touching was accidental. I find this claim to be untenable given my finding that he had unzipped the Daughter’s swimsuit and then rubbed her genital area skin-to-skin with his finger.[note: 29] These facts are wholly inconsistent with accident.

25     I therefore find that the Father had committed sexual abuse, and thus family violence, during the Swimming Pool Incident.

2 January 2022 – Plaza Singapura Incident

26     I next turn to the Plaza Singapura Incident.

Undisputed facts

27     At the material time, the Father’s access was supervised by C. The Father’s mother was also present. Tensions arose when he decided to drive the Daughter to another location (a mosque).

The Daughter’s evidence

28     The Daughter did not feel comfortable getting into the Father’s car because she feared that he might drive her somewhere else. When she refused to go with him, he picked her up by force, carried her towards his car, and tried to put her into it. She described being “very scared” and “full of panic” at the material time.[note: 30]

The video recording

29     C recorded the incident on video. The recording, which lasts about five minutes, shows the following.

(a)     The Father carrying the Daughter into the carpark while repeatedly saying, “Now is your time with Papa”.

(b)     The child appearing distressed. She struggled to free herself from the Father’s hold, reached out towards the Mother – who was walking behind them – and repeatedly cried out, “Mommy”. An unidentified female voice can be heard saying, “The child insists she wants to go down. Can you listen!”

(c)     The Father refusing to release the child and instead saying, “Now is the time for access. Don’t interfere!”

(d)     Upon reaching the car, a security officer arrived. The Daughter held onto the Mother’s hand. The Father said, “Let go, let go”.

(e)     The child repeatedly calling out for “Mommy”, struggling to be released, and crying, while the Father continued to prevent her from going to the Mother.

(f)     At one point, the Father lifting the child, forcing her into the car, and repeatedly saying, “After your time with Papa, Papa will send you back to Mom”. He also said, “Don’t cry”, while the child struggled to get out of his car.

(g)     An argument then ensued between the Father and the Mother. The Father asked the Daughter, “Can you come with Papa?” The child continued crying that she wanted her mother. The security officer said that “SPF is on the way” and told the Father not to force the child. The Father eventually released the child, and she returned to the Mother.

The Mother’s case

30     The Mother contends that the Father had committed the following acts which amount to family violence.

(a)     Physical abuse by wrongfully restraining the Daughter and attempting to confine her in his car against her will: s 58B(2)(b); and

(b)     Emotional or psychological abuse by causing the child distress: s 58B(4).

The Father’s case

31     The Father insists that he had the right to bring the Daughter to another location during his access period.[note: 31] He denies that having wrongfully restrained or confined her in his car. Further, he claims that he initially did not realise that she was distressed, and that her distress was caused instead by (a) C’s wrongful interference with his decision to bring her to a mosque, and (b) the Mother’s disruptive presence during access.[note: 32]

Finding – The Father had committed family violence

32     I accept that a parent has the authority to decide where to bring his/her child during an access period and to direct the child’s movements during that period. However, that authority is not absolute. It must be exercised reasonably and with due regard to the child’s emotional state: XPW v XPX [2025] SGFC 120 at [44(b)], [45] and [72(d)]. Access does not give a parent dominion over the child. The law does not sanction the use of physical force to compel a resisting child to accompany the parent – particularly where such conduct induces fear, distress, or emotional harm in the child.

33     In my judgment, the Father had crossed that line – by forcibly carrying and restraining the Daughter, and trying to force her into his car, against her will. He had persisted in such conduct despite her visible distress, repeated physical resistance, and the intervention of a security officer.

34     I find the Father’s claims – that he (a) initially thought that the Daughter was merely “playing the fool” and “laughing”, (b) did not know that she was distressed, and (c) had realised only later that she did not want to go with him[note: 33] – to be incredible and contradicted by the recording.

35     I also reject the Father’s attempt to shift blame for the Daughter’s distress to the Mother and C. The child’s distress was directly and substantially caused by his conduct.

(a)     At the material time, the child was already showing signs of unease in the Father’s company. A reasonable parent would have (i) given advance notice of his plan to proceed to the mosque and (ii) been open to C’s suggestion that she bring the child to the mosque separately.[note: 34]

(b)     The Father did not do any of these. Tensions escalated sharply after he declared abruptly that he wanted to bring the child to a mosque.[note: 35] This, together with his rough handling of the child and refusal to heed her pleas, substantially and directly caused her distress.[note: 36]

36     Accordingly, I find that the Father had committed (a) physical abuse by wrongfully restraining and attempting to confine the Daughter, as well as (b) emotional abuse by engaging in distressing conduct towards her. He therefore had committed family violence during the Plaza Singapura Incident.

16 January 2022 – First Paragon Mall Incident

37     Next, I come to the First Paragon Mall Incident.

Undisputed facts

38     This took place while the Father was having access supervised by C.

The Daughter’s evidence

39     According to the Daughter, she dreaded meeting the Father. She feared that something like the Swimming Pool Incident might happen again. She tried to avoid physical contact with him during their access, and did not like him touching her shoulder, even in public. During the hearing, she said that there was a video recording of the way the Father had touched her which had made her feel “really uncomfortable”.[note: 37]

C’s evidence

40     Additionally, C stated that during the access, the Father tried to talk to the Daughter about the “scratch” that the child had sustained and to tell her that it was caused by the Grandmother.[note: 38]

The Mother’s case

41     The Mother contends that the Father had committed emotional or psychological abuse against the Daughter by –

(a)     Discussing with the child her earlier allegation that he had touched her sexually in April 2019 (“April 2019 Incident”). In so doing, he had breached the terms of the child’s safety plan,[note: 39] and

(b)     Touching the child and thereby distressing her. The Mother relies on a video recording which shows (i) the Father touching the child’s right shoulder/upper arm area during access; and (ii) the child appearing to inch away from his touch.[note: 40]

Father’s case

42     The Father denies that any abuse occurred during the incident.[note: 41]

Meaning: “Emotional or psychological abuse”

43     Section 58B(4) of the Charter states that –

“Emotional or psychological abuse” means conduct or behaviour that —

(a)    torments, intimidates, harasses or distresses a person; or

(b)    causes or may reasonably be expected to cause mental harm to a person, including thoughts of suicide or inflicting self-harm.

44     On a plain reading, limbs (a) and (b) are complementary and are directed at different scenarios. Let me elaborate.

(a)     Limb (a) focuses on the nature of a respondent’s conduct. It is framed in action terms: “torments, intimidates, harasses or distresses”.

(b)     Limb (b), on the other hand, focuses on the consequences of the respondent’s conduct. It is concerned with conduct that causes, or may reasonably be expected to cause, mental harm. The Charter does not define “mental harm”. It however illustrates the concept by referring to thoughts of suicide or self-harm. These illustrations suggest that limb (b) is directed at sufficiently serious harm. Mere upset, annoyance, or distress, without more, will not ordinarily amount to “mental harm” within limb (b).

My decision

45     I find that the mere fact that the Father had discussed the April 2019 Incident with the Daughter does not establish emotional or psychological abuse. This is because when viewed objectively and in context, the evidence does not show that the discussion was conducted in a manner that tormented, intimidated, harassed, or distressed the child within s 58B(4)(a). Nor is there sufficient evidence that the discussion caused, or might reasonably be expected to cause, her mental harm within s 58B(4)(b).

46     As for the Father’s act of touching of the Daughter’s right shoulder or upper arm, I accept the child’s evidence – and the video lends some support – that she was uncomfortable and moved away. However, I am not satisfied that this incident, standing alone, crosses the threshold of emotional or psychological abuse under s 58B(4). The evidence establishes discomfort and avoidance. It does not establish tormenting, intimidating, harassing, or distressing conduct, or conduct that caused or might reasonably be expected to cause mental harm. A finding of “distress” will usually require something more than mere discomfort or unease. There is no evidence here that the child was visibly upset, fearful, or crying.

47     Accordingly, I find that the Mother has failed to prove that the Father had committed family violence during the First Paragon Mall Incident.

20 February 2022 – Second Paragon Mall Incident

48     I now turn to the Second Paragon Mall Incident. This too occurred during access supervised by C.

C’s evidence[note: 42]

49     During the access, C confronted the Father about his failure to return the Son to the Mother, who was the care-parent at the material time. Instead of addressing the matter, the Father said that he had been informed by the Police that he was allowed to use force against anyone who obstructed his access.

50     The Father’s response alarmed C. She observed that the Daughter, who had also heard the response, appeared distressed and uncomfortable. When the child tried to return to C’s side, the Father blocked her path. The child started to whimper and held onto C’s hand tightly. Around this time, the Mother arrived and said, “Just go”. C then pulled the child closer and they walked briskly away together. The Father followed them.

51     At one point, the Father grabbed the Daughter from C and carried her away. The child resisted by dragging her feet and clinging tightly to C’s hand. She also started crying. Despite the Mother pleading with him to stop, the Father continued to use his body to block both the Mother and C from reaching the child.

52     A store employee intervened to help the Mother and C. During the struggle, the Father used his elbow to hit the Mother’s arm and struck C on the chest. A commotion ensued, and the Police arrived shortly afterwards.

Video recording

53     C recorded the incident on video. It shows the following.

(a)     The Father holding onto the Daughter and using his body to block her from going to C.

(b)     The child trying to get away from the Father, clutching C’s hands, and attempting to return to her. The child appears to be upset.

(c)     The Father grabbing, restraining, and dragging the Daughter along as he walked.

Mother’s case

54     The Mother contends that the Father’s conduct amounted to family violence in that he had physically, emotionally, and psychologically abused the Daughter.

Father’s case

55     The Father disputes the Mother’s contention.

Meaning: Wrongfully restraining a person against the person’s will

56     Section 58B(2)(b) of the Charter provides that “physical abuse” includes “wrongfully ... restraining a person against the person’s will” (emphasis added). The Charter does not define the words “wrongfully” and “restraining”.

57     The ordinary meaning of “wrongfully” includes doing something in a manner that is unlawful. “Restraining” ordinarily refers to stopping someone from doing something, especially by using physical force. For completeness, I note that s 341 of the Penal Code 1871 (“Penal Code”) criminalises the act of “wrongful restraint”. Section 339 of the Penal Code defines that concept as voluntarily obstructing a person (A) so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which (A) has a right to proceed.

58     In my view, a respondent can be said to have “wrongfully ... restrain[ed] a person [(A)] against the person’s will” if, by physical force, the respondent prevents (A) from doing something or going somewhere which (A) is legally entitled to do.

59     In cases involving a young child, I am of the view that the concept of “wrongful restraint” demands a nuanced application. This is because in such cases, not every act of physical restraint is “wrongful”. A court must distinguish between two types of conduct.

(a)     The first is ordinary and proportionate parental control inherent in caring for a young child. This is implicit in s 58B(2), which recognises that “physical abuse” does not include “any force lawfully used ... by way of correction towards a child below 18 years of age”. Physical restraint, in this context, is not “wrongful”.

(b)     The second is coercive physical restraint that exceeds lawful parental control – particularly where it induces fear, distress, or emotional harm to the child. Such restraint is “wrongful” and amounts to family violence.

My decision

60     On the evidence, the Father’s conduct plainly falls into the second category. He was wrong (a) to have blocked the Daughter from returning to the Mother and C and (b) to have grabbed and dragged the crying child when she refused to go with him. His actions were rough, coercive, and unnecessarily distressing to the child.

61     Accordingly, I find that the Father had committed physical and emotional abuse against the Daughter, and thus family violence.

13 March 2022 – First Bedok Mall Incident

62     I turn to the next incident. This occurred during access, supervised by C, at Bedok Mall.

C’s evidence[note: 43]

63     Under the court order, the Father was supposed to return the Son to the Mother after the access on 9 March 2022. He had failed to do so.

64     During the access, C asked the Father about the return of the Son to the Mother’s care. He did not give a straight answer and began behaving aggressively. C felt that it was unsafe for the access to continue. She consulted the Daughter, who said that she felt uncomfortable and scared of the Father’s behaviour. Accordingly, C told the Father that she was terminating the access and then walked away with the child. He followed them. C decided to call the Mother for help. When the Mother arrived, she asked the Father to leave them alone.

65     C recorded a video of the incident. It shows that, despite the Mother’s request, the Father continued following them. He did so even when the child had repeatedly told him to “stop” and had said, “I want to go home”.

66     As the group tried to leave the mall, the Father suddenly walked in front of them and used his elbow to block their path. The child kept pleading with him to stop. The group eventually managed to get away from him.

Father’s case

67     The Father claims that the Daughter did not mean what she had said, and that she had told him to “stop” to show the Mother that she was rejecting him and to thereby please her. He further claims that the child had in fact wanted him to continue following them.

My decision

68     I reject the Father’s claims. They are inconsistent with the overall tenor of the interaction as shown in the recording. The evidence shows plainly that the child was harassed and distressed by him following her persistently.

69     Accordingly, I find that the Father had committed emotional abuse under s 58B(4)(a), and therefore family violence.

20 March 2022 – Second Bedok Mall Incident

70     I turn next to the supervised access at the Second Bedok Mall Incident.

C’s evidence[note: 44]

71     By the time of this access, 12 days had passed since the Father failed to return the Son to the Mother in breach of the court order. He had agreed to bring the Son to the supervised access and to return both children to the Mother after lunch.

72     However, the Father did not do as he had promised. When C – who had brought the Daughter to the supervised access – confronted him about this, the Father evaded the issue and became aggressive. His mother and the Son eventually joined them at the mall.

73     C decided to leave with the Daughter when the Father refused to allow the Mother to spend time with the Son after the access. In response, the Father blocked C with his body and prevented her from leaving. C then had a dispute with the Father’s mother over a remark she made to the Daughter.

My decision

74     I find that the Mother has failed to prove that the Father committed family violence against the Daughter during the Second Bedok Mall Incident. In coming to this finding, I am mindful that there was a dispute between the Father and C, and that he behaved aggressively towards C. However, there is insufficient evidence to show that he had committed physical, sexual, or emotional or psychological abuse against the Daughter during that access.

Summary of findings on Issue 1

75     In summary, I find that the Father had committed family violence against the Daughter on the following occasions (“Relevant Incidents of Family Violence”).

 

Date

Nature of violence committed

(1)

12.10.19

Swimming Pool Incident

Sexual abuse.

(2)

02.01.22

Plaza Singapura Incident

Physical and emotional abuse.

(3)

20.02.22

Second Paragon Mall Incident

Physical and emotional abuse.

(4)

13.03.22

First Bedok Mall Incident

Emotional abuse.



Issue 2: Is a protection order necessary?

76     Having found that the Father had committed family violence against the Daughter, the next question is whether a protection order is necessary.

The Mother’s submissions

77     The Mother submits that a protection order is necessary for the following reasons.[note: 45]

(a)     The Father has continued to engage in harmful conduct towards the Daughter.

 

Date

Nature of harmful conduct

(1)

From 2020 to August 2025

The Father had published various social media posts concerning, among other matters, the Daughter and the Swimming Pool Incident. (“Social Media Posts”)

(2)

Since 2018

The Father had turned up outside the Mother’s residence and shouted the Daughter’s name even when it was not his access day.[note: 46]

(3)

05.10.24

The Father turned up unannounced at the Daughter’s religious school and called for her. (“Religious Class Incident”)



(b)     According to the Mother –

110    The [Father] is not attuned to the adverse impact his presence, words, and actions have on [the Daughter]. He remains unapologetic and/or wilfully blind about the harm he has caused. He attempts to convince himself that [the Daughter] does not have any adverse feelings against him. This is dangerous. If the [Father] continues to delude himself that [the Daughter] is not averse to him, he will obviously continue to present himself to her every time he has an opportunity to do so without a care as to the mental anguish and psychological trauma he causes her. ...

The Father’s submissions

78     The Father submits that a protection order is unnecessary.[note: 47]

(a)     According to him, his conduct during the Relevant Incidents of Family Violence was caused by C, including her termination of access.

(b)     He denies the Daughter’s account of the Religious Class Incident. According to him, he did not enter the classroom to see her. He had simply entered the mosque to pass the Son a toy which the Son had wanted to give to the Daughter.[note: 48]

(c)     Apart from the Relevant Incidents of Family Violence, he had access to the Daughter on three occasions in January 2022 and on another occasion on 27 February 2022. There is no allegation of family violence on those occasions.

(d)     Further, he highlights that all this while, despite the absence of a protection order, he had not initiated contact with the child or attempt to remove her.

(e)     He alleges that for the past seven years, the Mother had frustrated his attempts to bond with the Daughter, and that this application is yet another attempt to alienate the child from him.

My decision

Risk assessment

79     An assessment as to whether a protection order is necessary is a forward-looking one. It involves asking whether there is a real risk that the respondent will commit family violence in future against the person sought to be protected. If the answer is yes, then a protection order is necessary: XFL v XFM [2024] SGFC 103 at [19] – [23].

80     In this case, I find that a protection order is necessary. These are my reasons.

Seriousness of family violence committed

81     The Father had committed sexual abuse. This is an egregious form of family violence as it constitutes a significant breach of trust by a parent to his child.

82     The gravity of the family violence is relevant to the necessity issue in two ways.

(a)      First, it informs risk. Minor, situational misconduct may have a low risk of recurrence once boundaries are clarified. However, grave misconduct which are intentionally committed – such as sexual abuse or coercive physical restraint – may reveal a conscious disregard for legal and personal limits and therefore a higher risk of recurrence.

(b)      Second, serious misconduct, such as sexual abuse, can have enduring psychological impact on its victims. Even if the risk of recurrence is uncertain, the fear of recurrence may itself impair the victim’s mental well-being. A protection order provides the assurance that the law stands between the victim and further harm.

83     Given the gravity of the Father’s misconduct, it is imperative that clear safeguards are put in place to ensure the Daughter’s safety in her future interactions with him.

Pattern of family violence committed

84     Such safeguards are especially critical given the Father’s pattern of aggressive conduct. He had physically restrained the Daughter and repeatedly disregarded her emotional state. I am concerned that even at the hearing, he insists that he is entitled to use coercive and unilateral force to ensure that access takes place.

85     These concerns are reinforced by the incidents referenced in [77] above. To be clear, I do not treat these incidents as proving additional acts of family violence. Instead, I consider them as evidence of (1) the Father’s pattern of refusing to accept boundaries; (2) his poor judgment in matters affecting the Daughter, and (3) the risk of future family violence. Let me elaborate.

(a)     It is not disputed that the Father had turned up in front of the Mother’s residence and called out the Daughter’s name. This evidence shows that he had initiated contact outside of court-approved access. The circumstances of such contact reflect on his failure to respect boundaries and the risk of future confrontations that can distress the child.

(b)     It is also not disputed that the Father had been publishing numerous social media posts to air his unhappiness with his access to the Daughter. In these posts, he had accused the Daughter of lying about the April 2019 Incident and the Swimming Pool Incident. He had also disclosed highly sensitive information, including (i) medical reports regarding the child’s genital area (prepared in connection with the two incidents) and (ii) information that can lead to her identification.[note: 49] These posts reveal that he has no qualm in taking extreme measures to secure child access.

(c)     Further, the Daughter claims that during one of her religious classes in October 2024, the Father had entered her classroom, called for her, and sought to speak to her. She panicked at the material time because she thought that he was going to take her away.[note: 50] I accept the Daughter’s evidence.[note: 51] This is yet another example showing that the Father is capable of taking intrusive measures and distressing the child.

Absence of recent family violence

86     The Father submits that there is no evidence of recent family violence against the Daughter and that this negates the necessity of a protection order. I disagree.

(a)      First, the absence of recent incidents can readily be explained by the fact that the child had refused to see him since 2023/2024.[note: 52] Once access stops, the opportunity for family violence will consequently be reduced.

(b)      Second, and importantly, the Father’s evidence at the hearing shows that he has not changed his position on how he intends to exercise access. For example –

(i)       He stated that, even if a court order provided for access at specific times and locations, he nevertheless considered himself entitled to attend the children’s schools at other times.[note: 53]

(ii)       He continued to justify the Social Media Posts and to maintain that they were not harmful to her.[note: 54] This is despite my explanation in XPX v XPW [2025] SGFC 80 (“Gag Order Decision”) that such a stance is plainly wrong.

(iii)       There is lingering doubt as to whether the Father has fully complied with the gag order. The evidence suggests that he may have retained the posts on social media and merely restricted their access to a narrower audience. According to him, he could comply with the gag order simply by changing the privacy settings on his accounts and keeping the posts online for “posterity”.[note: 55]

Lack of remorse

87     Further, I am deeply concerned by the Father’s persistent minimisation of the Daughter’s distress caused by his conduct. For instance, he has asserted that[note: 56] –

(a)     The child “was not ‘resistant’, and had no ‘distress reactions, fear and avoidance’ and/or ‘worries and fears’ towards him during his supervised access from 2019 up to 2023”.

(b)     The child’s “reported ‘psycho-emotional difficulties’, ‘feeling scared of father and worries about father doing “similar stuff” to her again, or father coming to her house to “shout and yell or to take (her) away”, “distress reactions, fear and avoidance towards father after the alleged sexual abuse incidences” and/or “worries or fears” in/since 2024 cannot have anything to do with me”.

88     I reject these assertions. The evidence overwhelmingly shows that the Daughter is fearful of the Father. This fear is well documented in contemporaneous records and is supported by the evidence of health and social care professionals. This fear is also apparent when she testified before me.

89     The Father’s continued denial of the Daughter’s distress is significant because it bears directly on the risk of family violence being committed in the future. A parent who refuses to recognise that his/her conduct harms the child is more likely to repeat it.

The Daughter is vulnerable

90     Finally, I note that since November 2024, the Father has been granted six hours of unsupervised access every alternate weekend. The nature of such access increases the opportunity and risks of future family violence. This is especially so since the Daughter is still young. She is only about 11 years old.

Summary of decision on the necessity issue

91     For the above reasons, I am satisfied that a protection order is necessary – both (a) to deter unreasonable behaviour by the Father and (b) to provide the assurance that the Daughter will be safe from harm during unsupervised access.

Issue 3: Are additional orders necessary?

Mother’s requests

92     In addition to a protection order, the Mother seeks (a) a counselling order, (b) a stay-away order, and (c) a no-contact order. She also requests that I call for a preliminary assessment report to determine whether a mandatory treatment order ought to be made against the Father.[note: 57]

93     I address each request in turn.

Counselling order

94     Section 60E of the Charter empowers a court to direct parties to attend counselling. It proceeds on the premise that structured engagement may change mindsets and thus facilitate safer future interactions.

95     In my judgment, arguments can be made both ways on whether counselling is appropriate in this case.

Case against making a counselling order

(a)     Counselling is ordinarily premised on at least some degree of insight, or at minimum a willingness on the part of the respondent to examine his own conduct. Where a respondent denies that he had committed family violence altogether, the basis for meaningful therapeutic engagement is weakened. In the present case, the Father has consistently maintained that he did nothing wrong and has continued to assert that he was entitled to enforce access by coercive means. This may suggest that the prospect of success is limited.

(b)     Further, the utility of a counselling order depends on how a respondent engages with the process. If the Father does not participate meaningfully, the order will achieve little. If he brings into the counselling sessions the same aggression and disregard for the Daughter’s emotional state that marked his conduct during access, the process may do more harm than good. This is especially so where such conduct is exhibited during joint counselling sessions (if any). If inappropriately managed, such sessions may aggravate (rather than alleviate) the child’s distress, and may expose her to further emotional harm.

Case for making a counselling order

(c)     On the other hand, the Father’s rigidity of position and resort to coercion are the very difficulties that counselling seeks to address. If there is to be any prospect of healthier interaction between Father and Daughter in future, some attempt ought to be made to confront and correct these underlying attitudes and behaviours. From this perspective, a counselling order may serve the child’s longer-term welfare by affording the Father an opportunity to change.

(d)     A counselling order carries legal consequences in the event of non-compliance. The counselling process can be structured in a manner responsive to the Daughter’s welfare, including by ensuring that the Father undergoes counselling separately from her.

96     The competing considerations are finely balanced. I cannot assume that counselling will succeed. Neither should I assume that the prospect of reform is foreclosed. In my judgment, therapeutic intervention should be given one further opportunity. I therefore make a counselling order. Whether this opportunity will bear fruit depends ultimately on the Father and the seriousness with which he chooses to engage the process.

Stay-away order

97     Under s 60B(2)(b) of the Charter, a court may prohibit a respondent from entering or remaining in specified places where such a restriction is necessary to protect a family member from further family violence. A stay-away order regulates proximity. Its suitability turns on whether a respondent’s physical presence presents a material risk to the protected person’s safety or psychological well-being.

98     I find that a stay-away order is necessary in this case.

(a)      First, the Father had committed family violence during access with the Daughter. He had disregarded her distress and maintained that he was entitled to enforce access unilaterally. As stated earlier, these findings show a marked inability or unwillingness to respect physical boundaries. In such circumstances, his proximity to the child becomes a risk factor.

(b)      Second, protection under the Charter is not confined to preventing physical harm. It extends equally to preventing emotional and psychological harm. The Father’s unexpected appearance during the Religious Class Incident had caused the Daughter to panic. A child who lives in fear of such sudden encounters cannot enjoy stability or security. A stay-away order will materially reduce that risk.

99     The Mother asks for an order restraining the Father “from visiting or remaining in an area outside the [Daughter’s] residence, her school, enrichment classes or religious classes, or any other place frequented by [the child]”.

100    My views on the Mother’s request are as follows.

(a)     To secure the Daughter’s security and welfare, it will be appropriate to grant a stay-away order which prohibits the Father from approaching within 100 metres of the child’s residence, school, enrichment classes and religious classes. This order is subject to the terms of (i) the Syariah Court Order (ORC-2024-002667) made on 27 November 2024 and (ii) any mutual agreement that may be made between the Father and the Mother from time to time.

(b)     I am not prepared to grant a stay-away order that prohibits the Father from visiting or remaining in “any other place frequented by [the child]”. Such an order is unduly wide, uncertain in scope, and thus unfair to the Father.

No-contact order

101    I next consider the Mother’s request for a no-contact order under s 60B(2)(c). Given my decision to grant both a protection order and a stay-away order, I considered whether a no-contact order is still necessary.

102    In addressing this issue, I note the following.

(a)     A protection order prohibits the commission of family violence. It however does not prohibit contact as such. A stay-away order regulates proximity to specified places. This order does not prevent communication. In other words, a protection order and a stay-away order do not prevent family violence from being committed through phone calls, messages, emails, or other remote means.

(b)     A no-contact order fills that gap. It prohibits a respondent from “visiting or communicating with” a protected person. In other words, it regulates interaction and communication, including by electronic means. It protects against family violence that may be inflicted without physical proximity.

103    In this case, the Father has repeatedly maintained that he is entitled to exercise access without restrictions or encumbrances. To this end, he had used various means to enforce access, including by turning up unexpectedly at the Daughter’s residence and religious class. It is not fanciful to think that he may resort to other means – including electronic means – to enforce access in future.

104    I therefore find that a no-contact order is necessary. To ensure proportionality and to avoid undermining or varying the existing access order, I order that the Father is prohibited from visiting, contacting, or communicating with the Daughter unless such contact is in accordance with the terms of (a) the Syariah Court Order (ORC-2024-002667) made on 27 November 2024 or (b) any mutual agreement that may be made between the Father and the Mother from time to time.

Mother’s request to call for a preliminary assessment report

105    I turn finally to the Mother’s request that I call for a preliminary assessment report.

Purpose of calling for a preliminary assessment report

106    The power to call for such a report is found in s 60F(6). It may be exercised before the court decides “whether reasonable grounds exist for the purpose of subsection (5)”.

107    Section 60F(5) sets out two preconditions for making a mandatory treatment order. It provides that, before making such an order –

... the court must have reasonable grounds to believe that –

(a)     X is likely to be suffering from a psychiatric condition; and

(b)    the psychiatric condition is likely to be a contributing factor for X’s conduct or behaviour that was the basis for making the protection order against X.

[emphasis added]

108    Seen in context, s 60F(6) assists a court in deciding whether there are reasonable grounds to believe that the preconditions in s 60F(5) exist.

Cases where it is not appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report

109    Next, I turn to the issue of when it is appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report.

110    In this regard, I am of the view that there are two situations where it would not be appropriate to call for such a report.

(a)      First, where the court has already formed the view that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the two preconditions in s 60F(5) exist (“Scenario 1”).

(b)      Second, where the court is satisfied that those preconditions clearly do not exist (“Scenario 2”).

111    In either scenario, there is no need to invoke s 60F(6). This is because there is no uncertainty as to whether the statutory preconditions exist.

(a)     In Scenario 1, the court must move to call for a formal assessment report: see s 60F(9).

(b)     In Scenario 2, the court will not proceed further since s 60F(5) requires “reasonable grounds” to exist before a mandatory treatment order may be considered.

Cases where it is appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report

112    Following from the above, one situation where it is appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report is where the court is in genuine doubt as to whether the two preconditions exist.

113    In my view, another situation where it is appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report is where the court has reasonable grounds to suspect that the preconditions in s 60F(5) may exist. (This is a lower threshold than “reasonable grounds to believe”.) For a case to come withing this situation, there must be some facts that would cause a reasonable person to suspect that the preconditions may be met. This is so even if the facts are incomplete. Once the court has reasonable grounds to suspect that the preconditions in s 60F(5) may exist, it would then be appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report. A specified psychiatrist can then provide professional views to assist the court in determining whether the preconditions are satisfied.

114    For completeness, I note that the calling of a preliminary assessment report is discretionary: s 60F(6)(a) (“may call”); XNY v XNZ [2025] SGFC 69 at [58]. Further, the exercise of that discretion is non-appealable: s 60F(8).

Basis for the Mother’s request

115    The Mother submits that it is appropriate to call for a preliminary assessment report because the Father had sexually abused the Daughter, and because of what she describes as his “explosive nature”, “abnormal conduct”, and “inability to discern right from wrong”. During oral submissions, her counsel clarified that by “abnormal conduct” and “inability to discern right from wrong”, she was referring to the Father’s misconduct set out at [75] and [77].

My decision

116    I do not accept that the matters raised by the Mother warrant the calling of a preliminary assessment report. These are my reasons.

(a)     The Father’s misconduct is undoubtedly serious. However, this consideration, by itself, does not constitute sufficient basis for inferring or suspecting that he is likely to be suffering from a psychiatric condition.

(b)     As explained earlier, there needs to be a proper basis before a preliminary assessment report may be called. No such evidential basis exists here. There is no evidence that the Father suffers from cognitive impairment, delusions, disordered thinking, or any other recognised indicia of mental illness. Nor is there any evidence linking his misconduct to a likely psychiatric condition.

(c)     In my view, the evidence points not to mental disorder, but to the Father’s poor judgment and unwillingness to acknowledge his wrongdoing. These are not symptomatic of cognitive impairment. They are more consistent with a person who insists on having his way and wants to evade responsibility for his misconduct.

117    For completeness, I also find that the preconditions in s 60F(5) are not made out. I do not have reasonable grounds to believe that the Father is likely to be suffering from a psychiatric condition, or that any such condition is likely to have contributed to the conduct that forms the basis of the protection order. It follows that this is not a case in which a mandatory treatment order is necessary.

118    Finally, I am satisfied that the suite of protective orders that I am making – namely, a protection order, counselling order, stay-away order, and no-contact order – directly and adequately address the risk of future family violence to the Daughter. These orders shall take effect

Conclusion

119    Before concluding, I wish to address the Father directly.

(a)     The protective orders do not deprive you of access to your daughter. Neither do they vary the terms of the existing access order.[note: 58]

(b)     The protective orders are necessary safeguards against family violence. Their purpose is to ensure that any future interaction between your daughter and yourself take place without coercion, fear, or abuse.

(c)     You need to remember three things. First, the orders impose clear and enforceable boundaries. They are to be obeyed. Second, an intentional breach carries serious consequences. You may be arrested and punished with a fine and/or imprisonment. Finally, you must not resort to family violence to enforce access or manage your conflicts. Abusive conduct will not be tolerated.

[note: 1]The Daughter was born in October 2014.

[note: 2]The Son was born in September 2011.

[note: 3]For completeness, while s 60A(1) also refers to cases where the respondent is “likely to commit” family violence, it is not necessary for me to reach that limb because the Mother’s case is advanced principally on past incidents.

tags: Probate and Administration Act 1934 – Validity of Will – Testamentary Capacity – Knowledge and Approval of Will – Undue Influence
title: XVQ v XVR
counsel: Plaintiff in Person
Mr Bhaskaran S/O Sivasamy (Messrs Skandan Law LLC) for the Defendant.
html:
XVQ v XVR

XVQ v XVR
[2025] SGFC 128

Case Number:FC/S 9/2023, HCF/DCA 93 of 2025
Decision Date:02 March 2026
Tribunal/Court:Family Court
Coram: Cassandra Cheong
Counsel Name(s): Plaintiff in Person; Mr Bhaskaran S/O Sivasamy (Messrs Skandan Law LLC) for the Defendant.
Parties: XVQ — XVR

Probate and Administration Act 1934 – Validity of Will – Testamentary Capacity – Knowledge and Approval of Will – Undue Influence

2 March 2026

District Judge Cassandra Cheong:

Introduction

1       The Plaintiff is the youngest of three sons of the deceased. The Defendant is the youngest sister of the deceased. This case involves a dispute between the parties over a Will executed by the deceased on 22 June 2020 ("the 2020 Will").

2       Under the 2020 Will[note: 1], the deceased revoked all prior Wills and codicils and appointed the Defendant as sole executrix and trustee of the said Will. She also made the following bequests:

(a)     to her second son, her sole immovable property (i.e. a HDB flat located at Klang Lane (“the HDB flat”);

(b)     to the Defendant all the assets listed in a table attached to the Will. These include mainly monies in bank accounts of the deceased (including monies in the account of an employment agency ran by the deceased) and some Singtel Shares;

(c)     $1.00 to each of her sons; and

(d)     the residuary estate to the Defendant.

3       It is undisputed that the 2020 Will was prepared by Mr A of A Law Corporation (“ALC”) and Ms C (“Ms C”), a former associate of the firm. It was subsequently executed by the deceased at the office of ALC, the witnesses of the said Will being the staff of the firm.

4       It is also undisputed that at the material time of execution of the 2020 Will, the Defendant was in Perth, Australia where she has been residing since 2005.

5       Prior to the execution of the 2020 Will, the deceased had executed two other Wills on 24 August 2018 (“the 2018 Will”) and 15 May 2019 (“the 2019 Will”) respectively.

The 2018 Will

6       Under the 2018 Will[note: 2], the deceased appointed the Defendant and a friend of the deceased as executors and trustees of the said Will. The deceased also made the following bequests:

(a)     The HDB flat to be held on trust by the trustees for the deceased’s granddaughter, P, until she attained 21 years of age;

(b)     After payment of her debts, testamentary and funeral expenses, all her ready cash, monies in her CPF, bank accounts, insurance policies, jewellery, furniture and fittings in the HDB flat, to be given to the Defendant; and

(c)     The sum of $1.00 to each of her three sons.

7       It is undisputed that the Deceased had independently engaged solicitors to draft the 2018 Will which she executed by affixing her left thumbprint on the said Will in the presence of two solicitors who acted as witnesses to the Will. The deceased then handed the said Will to the Defendant for safekeeping.[note: 3] Neither the Plaintiff nor the Defendant were involved in the preparation and execution of the said Will.

The 2019 Will

8       The 2019 Will[note: 4] was drafted by one Mr D (“Mr D”), a solicitor and long-time family friend of the deceased. Mr D first knew the deceased in 2015 when she approached him for assistance in relation to the Plaintiff’s divorce matters with his second wife. Mr D subsequently represented the Plaintiff in other legal matters, such as his custody application for P and represented him in his criminal matters as well.

9       As it was his practice not to act as a witness for any Will he had drafted, Mr D referred the deceased to another solicitor, Mr E (“Mr E”), for its execution. Mr E explained the will to the deceased in Tamil, who confirmed she understood the contents of the 2019 Will before affixing her left thumbprint onto the said Will in the presence of Mr E and another witness, M. Thereafter, the witnesses signed on the said Will in the presence of the deceased and each other.[note: 5]

10     Under the 2019 Will, the deceased appointed Mr D and the Plaintiff’s wife, B as executors and trustees of her will. She also made the following bequests:

(a)     The HDB flat to be held on trust for her granddaughter, P, until she attains 30 years of age upon which the trustees are to transfer the HDB flat to P absolutely. The deceased further stipulated that the trustees shall not rent out the flat under any circumstances and after the transfer of the flat to P, the flat shall not be sold and P must continue to stay at the property until her demise.

(b)     With regard to the deceased’s ready cash, monies in her CPF, bank accounts, insurance policies, jewellery, furniture and fittings in the HDB flat, these were also to be given to P.

(c)     The sum of $1.00 to each of her three sons.

(d)     The deceased further stated in the Will that no one else was allowed to enter her flat upon her demise, except for her trustees.

11     At the time of filing of his Affidavit of Testamentary Scripts dated 15 March 2022, the Plaintiff could only exhibit an unexecuted draft of the 2019 Will, which he had obtained from Mr D. A copy of the 2019 Will was subsequently obtained, with the assistance of Mr D, from Mr E and exhibited in the Plaintiff’s Supplementary Affidavit of Testamentary Scripts dated 20 May 2022. It is undisputed that the original 2019 Will has yet to be found. It is not in the possession of any of the parties.

12     According to Mr D, after the execution of the 2019 Will, the deceased entrusted the Will to him for safekeeping. The original Will was later returned to her upon her request, sometime in February 2020. Although no reason was given by the deceased for this, Mr D suspected that the deceased intended to execute a new Will.[note: 6]

13     The deceased subsequently passed away on 19 September 2021, alone at home. On the night of 18 September 2021, the Plaintiff’s wife made attempts to contact the deceased on her mobile phone but to no avail. Concerned with the wellbeing of the deceased, the Plaintiff’s wife, B went to the deceased’s home to check on her. When she arrived at the HDB flat, she called the deceased only to hear the sound of the deceased’s phone ringing inside the house without any response from the deceased. Worried she contacted the Plaintiff who suggested to call the police. B called the police and SCDF who forced open the door to the HDB flat. They found the deceased lifeless on the floor of her living room, due to a tragic fall that impacted her head.

14     On 16 February 2022, the Plaintiff commenced the present probate action in the Family Division of the High Court. It was subsequently transferred to the Family Justice Courts due to the value of the deceased’s estate. On 15 May 2023, after parties had filed and exchanged affidavits of testamentary scripts, the Plaintiff filed an amended Statement of Claim, claiming the following:

(a)     that the 2020 Will be declared void and invalid;

(b)     Probate to be granted to the executors appointed in the 2019 Will; and

(c)     The Defendant to provide full and detailed account of all the assets of the estate of the deceased, including the deceased's Australian property.

15     The Plaintiff disputes the validity of the 2020 Will on the following basis:

(a)     That the circumstances surrounding the 2020 Will were suspicious as the terms were suggested by the Defendant to the deceased prior to its execution. The changes to the 2019 Will were instructed by the Defendant, not the deceased, to ALC.

(b)     That the Will was executed pursuant to a misrepresentation by the Defendant to the deceased that the Defendant would hold the estate on trust for the benefit of the beneficiaries of the deceased's estate.

(c)     The deceased in reliance of the Defendant's misrepresentation, was unduly influenced, coerced and/or manipulated into executing the 2020 Will.

16     Initially, the Plaintiff had pleaded that even if the 2020 Will was valid, which he asserted was not, he was one of the beneficiaries of the deceased’s estate under a secret trust as established by the said Will. However, this claim was subsequently abandoned when the Plaintiff filed his Statement of Claim (Amendment No.2) on 15 May 2023.

17     The Defendant denied the Plaintiff’s claim on the basis that they were bare allegations and asserted that the deceased had exercised her judgement freely and independently when giving instructions to ALC and when executing the Will. Specifically, the Defendant denied making any representation to the deceased as alleged by the Plaintiff and further filed a counterclaim seeking the following:

(a)     A declaration that the Will dated 20 June 2020 is the true and last will of the deceased and that the Defendant is entitled to apply for, and be granted, probate over the estate of the deceased;

(b)     An order that the Plaintiff provide an account to the Defendant of the property of the deceased seized by the Plaintiff on 19 September 2021; and

(c)     Costs.

18     Although the Defendant disputed the execution of the 2019 Will in her Defence and Counterclaim (Amendment No.2), it is noted that during the trial and in her submissions, the Defendant expressly clarified that the execution of the 2019 Will was not disputed.

19     For avoidance of doubt, the deceased’s testamentary capacity is not disputed in the present case. Although the deceased had suffered a stroke sometime in 2012, based on the medical reports[note: 7] dated 21 December 2016 and 14 March 2017, the deceased was still able to function independently after her stroke, as evidenced by her continued care of P and the running of her employment agency until the date of her demise. It did not affect her testamentary capacity to execute a will. It is also not the Plaintiff’s case that the deceased lacked the requisite testamentary capacity to execute the 2020 Will.

20     After considering the evidence and the submissions made by the Plaintiff and counsel for the Defendant, I dismissed the Plaintiff’s claim as I found that his allegations were bare allegations, unsubstantiated by the evidence and granted order in terms of the Defendant’s counterclaim. Dissatisfied, the Plaintiff now appeals against the whole of my decision.

Background Facts

21     It is not disputed by parties that the deceased was a capable, independent woman of strong character. Her first marriage ended in 1980 after the deceased commenced divorce proceedings against her husband who had abandoned the family. Thereafter, the deceased became a single mother of three sons, whom she supported by working as a carpark attendant. She subsequently remarried in 1992 and helped her second husband to start his own scaffolding business. In 2004, the deceased started her own maid employment agency, specialising in bringing domestic helpers from India into Singapore, which she operated until her death on 19 September 2021. It was undisputed by parties that she was a successful businesswoman who was well known in Little India for her employment agency.

The deceased’s three sons

22     The deceased had three sons who were at one point or another, causes of distress and concern for the deceased. The first son, N, took over the deceased’s late husband’s business after the latter’s demise. Unfortunately, the business was mismanaged, incurring debts and requiring the financial assistance of the deceased. It was further alleged and undisputed by the parties that N had even forged the deceased’s signature and sold off two of her properties in order to pay off the said debts. The business, which the deceased’s late husband had painstakingly built, was eventually closed sometime in 2015 or 2016 and N declared an undischarged bankrupt.

23     The second son, S, has a history of drug related offences, including drug consumption and trafficking. In 1999, he was first sentenced to 10 years of imprisonment but was released in early 2004 or 2005 for good behaviour under a conditional remission order. After his release, he worked for his late step-father until 2008, when he was caught again for drug consumption and sentenced to 8 years jail. During this period of time, S turned over a new leaf. He completed his O-levels and A-levels and several computer courses. The deceased would visit him twice a month in prison. He also kept in touch with the Defendant, who had already migrated to Australia then, through written letters. S was subsequently released in February 2014 under a conditional remission order for good behaviour. At the time of the trial, he was gainfully employed since 2016 as a pharmaceutical driver for a pharmaceutical company. From 2014 to 2016, S lived together with the deceased and P at the deceased’s home, until he had an argument with the deceased over his drinking. S moved out thereafter to live with his then-girlfriend V, whom he subsequently married in 2020.

24     The Plaintiff is the youngest son of the deceased and from the evidence provided, arguably the son who gave the deceased the greatest cause for concern. He had a troubled youth. At the tender age of 12, he was placed in Boys Home for three years for causing grievous hurt to a prefect in his class. Around 15 years of age, he subsequently ran away from the home but was later reported by his parents and sent thereafter to a Reformative Training Centre (“the Centre”) for 4 years. At the Centre, the Plaintiff completed his N-levels. He was subsequently given early conditional release after 20 months and proceeded to obtain a diploma Certificate in culinary skills. Subsequently after that, the Plaintiff experienced 2 failed marriages, a series of failed businesses and convictions for other offences.

25     After his marriage to his first wife, the Plaintiff was convicted for stealing jewellery from his mother-in-law and sentenced to prison. This marriage ended in divorce. After the Plaintiff’s release from prison, he married P’s mother, who then gave birth to P. He was subsequently convicted for driving without a licence and for offences under the Employment Act. He was sentenced again to imprisonment from 2012 - 2014. He subsequently divorced P’s mother and in 2019, he married his current wife, B. The Plaintiff was last convicted for assaulting a CISCO Officer about 2 years ago.

26     The Plaintiff also started a series of businesses in the F&B industry, like a restaurant and minimart, and the construction industry. Some of these businesses were funded by the deceased’s late husband while he was alive. Unfortunately, most of these businesses proceeded to become struck off or dormant. As at the time of the trial, the Plaintiff was running a company in the business of transportation, manpower and construction.

Relationship between the deceased and her granddaughter P

27     It is not disputed by either party that the deceased cared deeply for her granddaughter P, who was the Plaintiff’s daughter from his second marriage. Pursuant to the Plaintiff’s divorce from his second wife, P lived with the deceased since she was 3 years old until she was 12 years old. The deceased took care of P and paid for all of P’s expenses solely. She even applied for the custody of P sometime in 2015. The Plaintiff did not take care of P.

28     During the period that P lived with the deceased, the deceased engaged tuition teachers to help P in her studies. She also enrolled P in dance classes, singing lessons and swimming lessons for P’s personal enrichment. The deceased was strict in her upbringing and would nag and scold P, who did not have an interest in studying or the extra-curricular lessons, resorting at times to using the cane to discipline P. As P grew older, P became increasingly defiant as she resented the strict approach of the deceased. This led to increased conflicts between the deceased and P.

29     The deceased was deeply troubled over the issue of who was to care and provide for P upon her demise, given the lack of parental involvement and guidance in P’s life, especially after her stroke in 2012. She subsequently executed the 2018 and 2019 Wills.

30     Sometime in 2019, the deceased found some of her jewelleries missing from her home. At the time, the only person living with the deceased at the HDB flat was P. The deceased suspected P of stealing her jewelleries upon the instigation of the Plaintiff and filed a police report. Upset by the deceased’s accusations, P left the HDB flat and stayed with the Plaintiff for two days. After which, she requested the deceased to let her return to the HDB flat to stay with her.

31     On 19 November 2020, P was brought to J Centre by Child Protection Services (“CPS”) and on 2 December 2020, she was placed on longer term care in J Centre (“the Centre”). The main reason for P’s admission into the Centre is unclear due to the lack of documentary evidence provided by the parties. From P’s oral testimony in court and from the Plaintiff’s affidavit, it appears that P had reported to her school teacher that she did not want to return home to live with the deceased, due to the constant nagging and scolding of the latter. P also threatened to harm herself if she was made to return home.[note: 8] Presumably, this led to P’s admission into J Centre. During her stay at the Centre, the deceased visited P almost on a weekly basis. She would bring lunch for P and leave after lunch was done, until the fateful day of 18 September 2021, when the deceased failed to turn up for her usual visit. P subsequently learnt on 19 September 2021 that the deceased had passed away.

Relationship between the deceased and the Defendant

32     The deceased was the 4th child among her 5 siblings, with the Defendant being the youngest. It is not disputed that the two shared a close relationship at the time of the deceased’s death. Even after the Defendant migrated to Australia in 2005, they remained in frequent communication and would talk almost every night on the phone. This is confirmed by P who lived with the deceased. The deceased would share with the Defendant, amongst other things, her woes over her three sons, the challenges she faced with P and seek the Defendant’s advice on what she should do about P. She also brought P to Australia to visit the Defendant sometime in 2016 and 2017.

33     As the deceased ceased her education after primary school, she would approach the Defendant, who completed her GCE ‘O’ Levels, to assist her in drafting correspondences to various government agencies and institutions and handling administrative matters on her behalf. For example, the Defendant assisted the deceased to draft correspondences to banks, income tax related enquiries and appeal letters relating to foreign domestic workers to the Ministry of Manpower. The Defendant also drafted correspondences on behalf of the deceased to the National Library Board for a lost library book and to the deceased’s landlord to extend the date given for early termination of her lease.

Circumstances leading up to the execution of the Will

34     Sometime around December 2019, the deceased informed the Defendant that she wanted to have a new will drafted and asked the Defendant to recommend a law firm.
