Applicable considerations

44     The considerations for assessing indirect contributions are well-established: see, e.g., XDZ v XEA [2024] SGFC 90 at [29(b)]. These include the following.

(a)     Payment of household expenses.

(b)     Performing household chores.

(c)     Caring for the child(ren) and other family members.

(d)     Expending money and effort to maintain/enhance the condition of the matrimonial home.

(e)     Expending money and effort to enhance the family members’ quality of life.

(f)     Making personal sacrifices for the family, e.g., stopping work to look after the child or so that a spouse may advance in his/her career and endeavors.

(g)     Providing support for the spouse. This can carry significant weight especially when the support was given during a spouse’s moment of personal crisis.

My decision

45     Considering the evidence and submissions, my findings on the parties’ relative indirect contributions are as follows.

 

Contributions

Findings

(a)

Payment of household expenses

The Husband had contributed more.

(a) The parties’ salaries are $13,842.18/month (Husband) and $3,300/month (Wife).

(b) It is not disputed that throughout the 24-year marriage, the Husband had all along had a higher income than the Wife and had thus contributed more towards the household expenses.[note: 45]

(b)

Performing household chores

(a) Neither party had provided concrete examples which show that one party had clearly contributed more than the other in this aspect of indirect contributions.

(b) The parties, who were working at all material times, had a domestic helper to assist in performing the household chores.

(c)

Caring for the children and other family members

The Husband had contributed more.

It is clear from the evidence of the older children (who are now adults) that[note: 46] –

(a) The Husband is the parent who has provided more care, concern and support for them.

(b) The Wife has a destabilizing effect in their lives.

(d)

Expending money and effort to maintain / enhance the condition of the matrimonial home

The Husband had contributed more.

The evidence shows that during the marriage, he had spent $186,810.65 on renovations to improve the family’s living conditions.[note: 47]

(e)

Expending money and effort to enhance the quality of life of family members

The Husband had contributed more.

He had –

(a) employed and paid for a domestic helper for the family,[note: 48]

(b) paid for the older children’s tertiary education,[note: 49] and

(c) purchased insurance policies for the children/naming them as the beneficiaries.[note: 50]

(f)

Making personal sacrifices for the family

Neither party had provided concrete examples which show that one party had clearly contributed more than the other in these aspects of indirect contributions.

(g)

Providing support for the spouse



46     Overall, I find that the Husband had clearly made greater indirect contributions to the well-being of the family. Adopting a broadbrush approach, I assess the parties’ relative indirect contributions to be 75% (Husband) and 25% (Wife).

Orders

50     Accordingly, I make the following orders.

(a)     The Husband shall make a cash payment of $126,112.65 to the Wife, being the Wife’s share of the pool of matrimonial assets less the value of her assets held in her name.[note: 51] Payment to the Wife shall be made within four months of this order.

(b)     The Wife’s rights, title, and interest in the matrimonial home shall be transferred (other than by way of sale) to the Husband upon the Wife making the full required CPF refunds to the Wife’s CPF account, within six months of the Final Judgment. The Wife shall bear the costs related to the transfer.

(c)     The parties' joint bank accounts, namely, the DBS savings account XXX414 and POSB savings account XXX251, shall be closed within four weeks of this order. The Husband shall be at liberty to retain the closing balances.

(d)     Each party shall retain all assets in their own name or joint names with others.
